issue_title: Control flow operators may fail when names of symbols given duplicate
issue_body:
The current implementation of control flow operators heavily relies the assumption that the name of symbols given should be different. This assumption is made in all three control flow operators: `foreach` ([here](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/symbol/contrib.py#L268), [here](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/symbol/contrib.py#L272), etc.), `while_loop` ([here](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/symbol/contrib.py#L471), [here](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/symbol/contrib.py#L490-L497), etc) and `cond` ([here](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/symbol/contrib.py#L632), [here](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/symbol/contrib.py#L651-L656), etc).

First, users could pass in a list of arbitrary symbols in which there are symbols sharing the same name. Second, I am not sure if cut graph could produce symbols with the same name. The worst thing is that it seems to me that control flow operators themselves produce symbols of the same name...

However, this is neither checked in the Python frontend, nor guaranteed in subsequent implementation. In `while_loop` and `cond`, this will trigger a `ValueError("The %d-th loop_var doesn't involve into the computation")`, which I happened to add to restrict unnecessary symbols which are unused in all subgraphs. I am not familiar of the behavior of `foreach` when it occurs.

issue_title: NDArrayIter expects "softmax_label"
issue_body:
This is not a problem yet, but if pull request #2531 gets approved people may have problems whenever they try build a model using a mxnet.symbol.SupportVectorMachine object as their top layer on Python.

Whenever we try to build a using NDArrayIter to save data and labels, the model fit will complain because it didn't found a "softmax_label". I worked around this on the example that is also on the pull request setting the attribute label again with

`train_iter.label = io._init_data(label, allow_empty=True, default_name='svm_label')`

But as I mentioned on #1810 that's a underlined function. That should be private, right?

Given that context what could be done to prevent people running on this problem?

issue_title: Training/testing only layers?
issue_body:
I am now trying to implement the paper:
https://arxiv.org/abs/1604.02878
which detects faces and regresses their bounding boxes at the same time. 
For BB regression, I use L_2 distance metric based loss, but in testing I want to use different evaluation metric rather than simply using L_2 distance. For that I want to use some intermediate layers within the network but I found that I can only use the outputs from get_outputs() function in evaluation. Caffe, for instance, has the "phase" keyword so that the network structure can vary between training and testing phases. Dose mxnet have a similar functionality?

issue_title: [PROPOSAL] SparseNDArray Python API
issue_body:
# Overview

End-to-end design overview available at https://github.com/dmlc/mxnet/issues/5498
**This issue is opened to gather comments regarding priority of sparse operators to support.**

#### Data Types

We focus on two sparse formats: csr and row-sparse. Both symbolic and imperative execution will be supported. Implementation on CPU has higher priority than GPU.

#### Implicit Sparse/Dense Format Conversion

Since not all operators are re-implemented with sparse input, sometimes a sparse tensor is converted to other format if needed. Specifically,  
* If an operator doesn't handle sparse input, then sparse inputs are converted to dense ones automatically on runtime. 
* By default an operator refuses to handle inputs with multiple sparsity formats. In the future we might consider convert them to a same format, chosen by the operator.

Warnings will be thrown so that users are aware of such conversion.

# Python API

## Array Creation Routines

#### mx.sparse_nd.csr_mat(dense_mat, ctx=None, dtype=None)
Creates a `CSR` matrix from a dense matrix.
#### mx.sparse_nd.csr_mat(data, indices, indptr, shape, ctx=None, dtype=None)
Creates a `CSR` matrix with standard input.
```
>> data = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6])
>> indices = np.array([0, 2, 2, 0, 1, 2])
>> indptr = np.array([0, 2, 3, 6])
>> shape = (3, 3)
>> result = mx.sparse_nd.csr_mat(data, indices, indptr, shape)
   <SparseNDArray 3x3@cpu(0)>
>> result.asnumpy()
   array([[1, 0, 2],
         [0, 0, 3],
         [4, 5, 6]], dtype=float32)
```
#### mx.sparse_nd.row_sparse_array(data, indices, shape, ctx=None, dtype=None)
Creates a `row sparse` array, which is sparse in the first dimension, the dense in the rest(similar to IndexedSlice in tf). Row sparse array is not limited to 2D.
```
>> indices = np.array([0, 2])
>> data = np.array([[1, 0, 2], [3, 4, 5]])
>> shape = (3, 3)
>> result = mx.sparse_nd.row_sparse_array(data, indices, shape)
   <SparseNDArray 3x3@cpu(0)>
>> result.asnumpy()
   array([[1, 0, 2],
         [0, 0, 0],
         [3, 4, 5]], dtype=float32)
```

## Array Convertion
#### SparseNDArray.to_csr()
Makes a copy of data and returns SparseNDArray in CSR format.

#### SparseNDArray.to_row_sparse()
Makes a copy of data and returns a SparseNDArray in row sparse format.

#### SparseNDArray.to_dense()
Returns a dense NDArray

#### SparseNDArray.asnumpy()
Returns a dense numpy.ndarray object with value copied from this array.

## Mathematical Functions
*For common functions, their descriptions are omitted.*
#### mx.sparse_nd.dot(lhs, rhs, transpose_a=False, transpose_b=False, out=None)
Ordinary dot product of two arrays. 
```
>> # create a sparse array
>> a = mx.sparse_nd.csr_mat(..., shape=(10,10)) 
>> 
>> # create a dense array 
>> b = mx.nd.array(..., shape=(10, 1)) 
>> 
>> # sparse - dense matrix multiplication, returns a dense ndarray
>> mx.sparse_nd.dot(a, b) 
>> 
>> # sparse - sparse matrix multiplication, returns a dense ndarray
>> mx.sparse_nd.dot(a, a)
```
#### mx.sparse_nd.sum 
#### mx.sparse_nd.add 
#### mx.sparse_nd.subtract 
#### mx.sparse_nd.multiply 
#### mx.sparse_nd.divide 
#### mx.sparse_nd.negative 

## Symbol

#### mx.symbol.Variable
Variable with attr['sparse_type'] will be considered as sparse input. 
#### mx.symbol.Embedding
Embedding layer with sparse input.

#### mx.symbol.dot
#### mx.symbol.sum
#### mx.symbol.broadcast_add
#### mx.symbol.broadcast_sub
#### mx.symbol.broadcast_mul
#### mx.symbol.broadcast_div
#### mx.symbol.negative

## Array Manipulation Routines
#### SparseNDArray.transpose()
Permute the dimensions of an array.

## Miscellaneous Functions
#### SparseNDArray.check_format()
Check whether the matrix format is valid

## Serialization
#### mx.sparse_nd.load
#### mx.sparse_nd.save

## KVStore and Distributed Training
User is not expected to change kv-store code to have sparse gradient updates. Once the engine detects sparse gradients, their parameters are updated using sparse gradient accordingly.


@tqchen @jermainewang Any comment on introducing a new SparseNDArray front-end class?
@mli

issue_title: Restrict running some operators on only one GPU?
issue_body: Is it possible to restrict some operators to run on only one GPU? For example, pytorch calculate the Loss on only one GPU. This benefits some losses like Triplet Loss, where we need to search hard positives and hard negatives. The search space is N-GPU times larger.

## Minimum reproducible example

```python
import mxnet as mx
import random
import sys

#parameter to break this code or not
break_code = sys.argv[1].lower() == "true"

#synthetically create 1000 encoded sentences
encoded_sentences = []
for i in list(range(1000)):
    sentence_length = random.randint(1, 20)
    sentence = random.sample(range(20), sentence_length)
    encoded_sentences.append(sentence)

#define hyperparameters/info
vocab = list(set(index for list in encoded_sentences for index in list))
batch_size = 18
num_embed = 10
num_hidden = 3
buckets = [5, 10, 15, 20]
epochs = 15

# Create and register a custom initializer
@mx.init.register
class WeightInit(mx.init.Initializer):
    def __init__(self):
        super(WeightInit, self).__init__()
    def _init_weight(self, _, arr):
        #set all weights to 1
        arr[:] = 1
        #scale the weight for not entity values
        arr[:,5,:] /= 5
    def _init_bias(self, _, arr):
        arr[:] =1

#use mxnet bucketing iterator, label is next value in sentence
train_iter = mx.rnn.BucketSentenceIter(sentences=encoded_sentences,
                                       batch_size=batch_size,
                                       buckets=buckets,
                                       data_name='data',
                                       label_name='softmax_label')

#define recurrent cell
r_cell = mx.rnn.LSTMCell(num_hidden=num_hidden)

#architecture is defined in a function, to allow variable length input sequences
def sym_gen(seq_len):
    """function that creates a network graph, depending on sequence length"""

    #define data shapes
    data_shape = (batch_size, seq_len)
    label_shape = (batch_size, seq_len)

    data = mx.sym.Variable('data')
    print("\ndata shape: ", data.infer_shape(data=data_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    label = mx.sym.Variable('softmax_label')
    print("\nlabel shape: ", label.infer_shape(softmax_label=label_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    #initialize weight array for use in our loss function, using a custom initializer and prevent the weights from being updated
    label_weights = mx.sym.BlockGrad(mx.sym.Variable(shape=(1, len(vocab), 1), init=WeightInit(), name='label_weights'),name = "blocked_weights")
    broadcast_label_weights = mx.sym.broadcast_to(label_weights, shape = (batch_size, len(vocab), seq_len), name = 'broadcast_weights')
    print("\ninput weights shape: ", broadcast_label_weights.infer_shape()[1][0])

    embed = mx.sym.Embedding(data, input_dim=len(vocab), output_dim=num_embed,name='embed')
    print("\nembed layer shape: ", embed.infer_shape(data=data_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    output, shapes = r_cell.unroll(seq_len, inputs=embed, merge_outputs=True)
    print("\nconcatenated recurrent layer shape: ", output.infer_shape(data=data_shape)[1][0], "after ", seq_len, " unrolls\n")

    reshape = mx.sym.Reshape(output, shape=(-1, num_hidden))
    print("\nafter reshaping: ", reshape.infer_shape(data=data_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    fc = mx.sym.FullyConnected(reshape, num_hidden=len(vocab), name='pred')
    print("\nfully connected layer shape: ", fc.infer_shape(data=data_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    label = mx.sym.Reshape(label, shape=(-1,))
    print("\nlabel shape after reshaping: ", label.infer_shape(softmax_label=label_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    onehot_label = mx.sym.one_hot(indices=label, depth=len(vocab), name='one_hot_labels')
    print("\none hot label shape: ", onehot_label.infer_shape(softmax_label=label_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    sm = mx.sym.softmax(fc, name='predicted_proba')
    print("\nsoftmax shape: ", sm.infer_shape(data=data_shape)[1][0], "\n")

    if break_code == False:
        loss = -((onehot_label * mx.sym.log(sm)) + ((1 - onehot_label) * mx.sym.log(1 - sm)))
    else:
        loss = -((onehot_label * mx.sym.log(sm)) + ((1 - onehot_label) * mx.sym.log(1 - sm))) * broadcast_label_weights

    print("\ncross entropy loss shape: ", loss.infer_shape(data = data_shape, softmax_label=label_shape)[1][0])

    loss_grad = mx.sym.MakeLoss(loss, name = 'loss')
    print("\nloss grad shape: ", loss_grad.infer_shape(data=data_shape, softmax_label=label_shape)[1][0])

    return loss_grad, ('data',), ('softmax_label',)

#create a trainable bucketing module on cpu
model = mx.mod.BucketingModule(sym_gen=sym_gen, default_bucket_key=train_iter.default_bucket_key, context=mx.cpu())

#fit the module
metric = mx.metric.create('loss')
model.bind(data_shapes=train_iter.provide_data,
           label_shapes=train_iter.provide_label)
model.init_params()
model.init_optimizer(optimizer='Adam')
for epoch in range(epochs):
    train_iter.reset()
    metric.reset()
    for batch in train_iter:
        model.forward(batch, is_train=True)             # compute predictions
        model.backward()                                # compute gradients
        model.update()                                  # update parameters
        model.update_metric(metric, batch.label)        # update metric
    print('\n', 'Epoch %d, Training %s' % (epoch, metric.get()))
```

## Steps to reproduce/solve

1. `python mwe.py false` to train successfully
2. `python mwe.py true` to reproduce error

issue_title: L2 norm for fp16 goes to nan, needs to be fixed
issue_body:
When .norm() method is called on a float16 array it tries to compute the norm in fp16, which causes the squared sum to go out of range and causes nan as output. Large normalizations should be kept in float32, similar to how it's done for softmax and batchnorm. Ideally this should be done by accumulating the squared sum into a float32 buffer. 
This problem might affect the L2 norm operator as well. Haven't verified that.

issue_title: I use multilayer bidirectional LSTM and CTC to train, but Train-Accuracy is 0
issue_body:
Here is my code

`
def bi_lstm_unroll_concat_first(seq_len, num_hidden, num_label, num_lstm_layer):
    forward_param_cell = []
    backward_param_cell = []
    forward_last_states = []
    backward_last_states = []

    for i in range(num_lstm_layer):

        forward_param_cell.append(LSTMParam(i2h_weight=mx.sym.Variable("forward_l%d_i2h_weight" % i),
                                     i2h_bias=mx.sym.Variable("forward_l%d_i2h_bias" % i),
                                     h2h_weight=mx.sym.Variable("forward_l%d_h2h_weight" % i),
                                     h2h_bias=mx.sym.Variable("forward_%d_h2h_bias" % i)))
        forward_state = LSTMState(c=mx.sym.Variable("forward_l%d_init_c" % i),
                          h=mx.sym.Variable("forward_l%d_init_h" % i))
        forward_last_states.append(forward_state)

        backward_param_cell.append(LSTMParam(i2h_weight=mx.sym.Variable("backward_l%d_i2h_weight" % i),
                                            i2h_bias=mx.sym.Variable("backward_l%d_i2h_bias" % i),
                                            h2h_weight=mx.sym.Variable("backward_l%d_h2h_weight" % i),
                                            h2h_bias=mx.sym.Variable("backward_%d_h2h_bias" % i)))
        backward_state = LSTMState(c=mx.sym.Variable("backward_l%d_init_c" % i),
                                  h=mx.sym.Variable("backward_l%d_init_h" % i))
        backward_last_states.append(backward_state)

    data = mx.sym.Variable('data')
    label = mx.sym.Variable('label')
    wordvec = mx.sym.SliceChannel(data=data, num_outputs=seq_len, squeeze_axis=1)

    hidden = [wordvec[i] for i in range(seq_len)]
    forward_hidden = range(seq_len)
    backward_hidden = range(seq_len)

    for layeridx in range(num_lstm_layer):

        for seqidx in range(seq_len):

            forward_next_state = lstm(num_hidden, indata=hidden[seqidx],
                              prev_state=forward_last_states[layeridx],
                              param=forward_param_cell[layeridx],
                              seqidx=seqidx, layeridx=layeridx)

            forward_hidden[seqidx] = forward_next_state.h
            forward_last_states[layeridx] = forward_next_state

            k = seq_len - seqidx - 1
            backward_next_state = lstm(num_hidden, indata=hidden[k],
                              prev_state=backward_last_states[layeridx],
                              param=backward_param_cell[layeridx],
                              seqidx=k, layeridx=layeridx)

            backward_hidden[k] = backward_next_state.h
            backward_last_states[layeridx] = backward_next_state

        for seqidx in range(seq_len):

            hidden[seqidx] = mx.sym.Concat(*[forward_hidden[i], backward_hidden[i]], dim=1)

    hidden_concat = mx.sym.Concat(*hidden, dim=0)

    pred = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=hidden_concat, num_hidden=70)

    label = mx.sym.Reshape(data=label, shape=(-1,))
    label = mx.sym.Cast(data = label, dtype = 'int32')
    sm = mx.sym.WarpCTC(data=pred, label=label, label_length = num_label, input_length = seq_len)

    return sm
`

issue_title: Add --pdb and --ipdb options to runserver_plus / all commands?
issue_body:
There is https://github.com/tomchristie/django-pdb, which adds additional options to `runserver` (which does not appear to work in Django 1.7 anymore).

It uses a middleware to start pdb/ipdb in case of errors.

I think this would be a nice addition to django-extensions.

It would be even better, if these options could be added to all management commands somehow globally (requested for django-pdb in https://github.com/tomchristie/django-pdb/issues/19).

issue_title: Fire all triggers on multiple matches?
issue_body:
I was surprised by this behavior: 

```
$ watchman watch-project test/
$ watchman -- trigger /tmp/test js_first '*.js' -- ls -l
$ watchman -- trigger /tmp/test js_second '*.js' -- ls
$ touch /tmp/test/foo.js
```

Inspection of the Watchman log shows that only the first trigger, js_first, was fired. If I unregister the first trigger, then the second one fires. 

Would it be possible to have a mode where all matching triggers are fired, or is that prohibitively difficult or expensive for some reason?

issue_title: sitemap.xml.gz fails
issue_body:
If pa.multilingual is installed, generating the "/sitemap.xml.gz" File fails silently, because "LIF", "LRF" are not indexed or searched
Only Plone Root (ex. /Plone) will be shown in the xml

Steps to reproduce:
- working (without pa.multilingual)
  - Install new Plone
  - Go to Website Configlet, activate "sitemap.xml.gz"
  - open, download and view "sitemap.xml.gz"
  - it should be contain ../news, ../events, and Members - the default content
- not working (with pa.multilingual)
  - Install new Plone
  - Install pa.multilingual
  - (Test if LIF and LRF are enabled in "Search"-Configlet)
  - open, download and view "sitemap.xml.gz"
  - It contains only our Plone-Root

Plone Versions:
Plone 4.3.4
pa.multilingual 2.0.1
plone.app.layout 2.3.12

Plone 5.0
pa.multilingual 3.0.12
plone.app.layout 2.5.15

issue_title: EXTINF tag with optional comma?
issue_body: m3u8 library requires EXTINF tag to have title, but the title is optional, see https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-pantos-http-live-streaming-09#section-3.3.2

issue_title: Check complexity of addon entrypoints
issue_body:
It is a general good practice to keep the entrypoints (the python files refered in addon.xml) to a minimum since they won't get compiled to bytecode. Usually the entrypoint should be something as simpler as:

```python
import resources.lib.plugin

plugin.run()
```

While the main plugin code is kept in a module.
The `addon-check-tool` could somehow check the complexity of the entrypoint and might suggest the author to improve the addon performance by pointing to an example. A few possibilities:

1) Just check the number of uncommented lines in the entrypoint file and decide if it is lower than, lets say, 10 lines?

2) Check the number of imports on the entrypoint? This probably means the main plugin logic is executed there or, at least, there is room for improvement

3) Something more complex by exploiting cyclomatic complexity? Might be too hard to accomplish.

4) Infer from the number of python files in the addon that are not the entrypoint. This can lead to a lot of false positives though.

Option 1) seems the easiest one to implement if reforced by a few more checks

issue_title: Work around the celery/rabbitmq shortcomings
issue_body:
Transferring large amount of data with celery/rabbitmq is an issue. It is easy to run out of memory when unpickling large arrays, and also we can have rabbitmq block or run out of mnesia space. Moreover, the performance is far from optimal. On the other hand, we discovered that reading the data via NFS works perfectly well. This is why a few months ago we introduced a configuration parameter `shared_dir` pointing to a NFS mounted directory readable by all workers. There is no calculator taking advantage of this feature yet.

Here is the proposal: let's introduce tasks able to read directly from the shared directory and let's add an attribute `shared_dir_on` to such tasks. The value of `shared_dir_on` is a boolean flag, true if the `shared_dir` is defined in the configuration file, false otherwise. Then the distribution logic will be the
following:

1. if `shared_dir_on` is true, use celery if `use_celery` is true (i.e. nothing strange)
2. if `shared_dir_on` is false, use `concurrent.futures` even if `use_celery` is true, but print a warning

In this way the tasks will run even on clusters where there is no `shared_dir`, only the computation will be slower, since it will use the cores of the controller node and not the other machines.

This is the best possible solution, since the alternative is having the calculation fail because of excessive data transfer (if it is large; if it is small it is not an issue to run it on the controller node only).

issue_title: dpkg-genchanges fails if git commit starts with 'b'
issue_body:
```
make[1]: Leaving directory `/home/ubuntu/build-deb'
   dh_md5sums -O--buildsystem=python_distutils
	(cd debian/python-oq-engine >/dev/null ; find . -type f ! -path "./etc/supervisor/conf.d/openquake-webui.conf" ! -path "./etc/supervisor/conf.d/openquake-dbserver.conf" ! -path "./etc/supervisor/conf.d/openquake-celery.conf" ! -path "./etc/openquake/openquake.cfg"  ! -regex './DEBIAN/.*' -printf '%P\0' | LC_ALL=C sort -z | xargs -r0 md5sum > DEBIAN/md5sums) >/dev/null
	chmod 644 debian/python-oq-engine/DEBIAN/md5sums
	chown 0:0 debian/python-oq-engine/DEBIAN/md5sums
   dh_builddeb -O--buildsystem=python_distutils
	dpkg-deb --build debian/python-oq-engine ..
dpkg-deb: building package `python-oq-engine' in `../python-oq-engine_2.0.0-0~trusty01~dev1466173780+b868218_all.deb'.
 signfile python-oq-engine_2.0.0-0~trusty01~dev1466173780+b868218.dsc

 dpkg-genchanges  >../python-oq-engine_2.0.0-0~trusty01~dev1466173780+b868218_amd64.changes
dpkg-genchanges: error: cannot read ../python-oq-engine_2.0.0-0~trusty01~dev1466173780.dsc: No such file or directory
dpkg-buildpackage: error: dpkg-genchanges gave error exit status 2
```

For an unknown reason `dpkg-genchanges` looks for `python-oq-engine_2.0.0-0~trusty01~dev1466173780.dsc` instead of `python-oq-engine_2.0.0-0~trusty01~dev1466173780+b868218.dsc.dsc:`.
This happens only if the git commit starts with *+b*. The bug is in `dpkg-buildpackage` and not on our side.

See https://ci.openquake.org/job/master_oq-engine/2522/consoleFull

How to reproduce manually:
* On ubuntu 14.04
* Clone oq-engine and install the build dep
* In `debian/changelog` change the version name accordingly
* From `oq-engine` run `dpkg-buildpackage`

We should report the bug to debian/ubuntu.

issue_title: [Feature request] Ability to fetch full cloudwatch logs from boto after lambda invoke
issue_body:
I would really like to be able to fetch cloudwatch logs from boto3. 
As in, I want a way to fetch in python what I can see at:

    https://ap-southeast-2.console.aws.amazon.com/cloudwatch/home?region=ap-southeast-2#logEventViewer:group=/aws/lambda/my_lambda_name;start=PT5M

As far as I can tell, there's no way to do this with boto3.

I can get close by looking at `base64.b64decode(response['LogResult'])` after calling `response = boto3.client('lambda', config=config).invoke(...)`.

However those logs are only a subset of the actual logs.

The specific case I'm dealing with is looking at logs of lambdas which execute python `nose` tests. In the web GUI for CloudWatch I see:

* Traceback (exception captured, handled and printed by nose, I don't know exactly how);
* Logger output; then
* stdout

With `base64.b64decode(response['LogResult'])` I only see logger output, not the other 2.

Is there a way to see the other 2 from boto3? If not, I would like there to be. My current workaround is to run `client.invoke()` and then generate the aforementioned url so the user can copy and paste it into their browser. This is a pain because (aside from being manual), when multiple lambdas are invoked in parallel, the logs become very confusing, and it takes a while to find what I'm actually looking for.

In addition to modifying `client.invoke` so that it returns the full logs, I think it would be generally useful to add a function to the CloudWatch client so that you can look up the logs for a particular lambda, or a particular invocation of a particular lambda.

issue_title: Update vendored requests library to improve performance
issue_body:
As I was profiling one of our applications, I noticed that instantiating a new boto session was taking a significant amount of time (on AWS Lambda request with cold cache it was above 100ms), and I traced it down to the way requests determines the default user agent. This was fixed in 2.8.0 ([changelog](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/master/HISTORY.rst#280-2015-10-05), [actual diff](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/commit/2a635ba87d7c7c3ef75e7f2013a2cd1a207a0e1a)).

Could you update the vendored requests library's version to at least 2.8.0? I'd be happy to contribute a patch if you agree that this is a good idea.

issue_title: status_code requires __ne__ to work in python 2.7.x
issue_body:
Quoting to the 2.7.x data model [1]

"There are no implied relationships among the comparison operators. The truth of x==y does not imply that x!=y is false. Accordingly, when defining __eq__(), one should also define __ne__() so that the operators will behave as expected."

So in python 2.7.x one cannot do the following

browser = Browser()
browser.visit("http://www.google.com")
if browser.status_code != 200:
    print("ERROR")

This however will work in python 3.x because the data model says __ne__ will automatically invert the return code of __eq__

[1] https://docs.python.org/2/reference/datamodel.html

1. Get the track using the Spotify Console ([link](https://beta.developer.spotify.com/console/get-track/?id=1tRoJfwQ2upkIVEVKkuPcT&market=)).

Are there any ordering rules applied by Mopidy-Spotify when the array of artists is returned? Or perhaps caching of artists affects the return? Let me know if I can do any further testing to help identify the issue.

issue_title: Better project introduction to PyPI
issue_body:
The current introduction just says:

```
"""Selenium2Library is a web testing library for Robot Framework that leverages the Selenium 2 (WebDriver) libraries."""
```

It would be great to have some more information, including installation instructions there. A very easy and DRY solution is reading the current README.rst content to long_description in setup.py. We recently did that with StatusChecker project and it works great. The same information is visible in [BitBucket project pages](https://bitbucket.org/robotframework/statuschecker/src) (GitHub obviously shows READMEs same way) and on [PyPI pages](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/robotstatuschecker), and the [implementation](https://bitbucket.org/robotframework/statuschecker/src/master/setup.py) was pretty trivial.

issue_title: post_detail.html: meta_description/meta_keywords vs. base.html:meta
issue_body:
What's about [post_detail.html#L4f](https://github.com/nephila/djangocms-blog/blob/develop/djangocms_blog/templates/djangocms_blog/post_detail.html#L4)?:
```
{% block meta_description %}{{ post.meta_description }}{% endblock meta_description %}
{% block meta_keywords %}{{ post.meta_keywords }}{% endblock meta_keywords %}
```
What's the idea of that and how is it intended to be "used"?

There's also `{% block meta %} ... {% include "meta/meta.html" %}` in [base.html#L3ff](https://github.com/nephila/djangocms-blog/blob/develop/djangocms_blog/templates/djangocms_blog/base.html#L3).

By including `{% block meta %}{% endblock meta %}` in my page skeleton template, meta tags are rendered correctly, but the blocks of `post_detail.html` are pretty useless.

Whereas when including the two individual blocks instead of the `meta` one, the block in `base.html` is useless (except for further meta like social media stuff). But, big point, the contents of the two individual blocks is just the meta content payload, without `<meta` tag and `name` attr...

I guess, this should be cleaned up somehow...
...resolve this duplication.
Whereas the blocks from `post_detail.html` don't work just like that. If, then they should maybe read something like this:
```
{% block meta_description %}
    <meta name="description" content="{{ post.meta_description }}" />
{% endblock meta_description %} 
```

PS:
I stepped into this because I wanted to include the tags into the meta-keywords as well (having something of this form:
```
{% block meta_keywords %}
    <meta name="keywords" content="{{ post.meta_keywords }},{{ post.tags.names|join:',' }}" />
{% endblock meta_keywords %} 
```
(the hardcoded ',' in between is not very clean...)
If (as a solution to this issue) the two concerned lines from `post_detail.html` were removed in the project repo, it would be harder for the user to come up with a solution to my problem-case depicted above...

issue_title: Proposal: Explicit command to create a Pipfile in the current directory
issue_body:
Pipenv does not currently have an explicit “new project” command, but automatically ensures everything is in order when you run something. While this works well in general, it may leave people in the dark if the environment configuration is slightly off, as is the case of #2185.

#2185 is obviously a user error, and #2186 would help identify the problem, but the user, unless with substantial understanding to Pipenv internals, would still not be able to understand what action should be taken next.

A similar, but non-user error situation is to create nested Pipfile projects. This is suggested in #2148, which introduces the notion of “including” sub-projects. How this inclusion mechanism should be handled, if at all, is up to further discussion, but the idea of a Pipfile sub-project in itself is currently already problematic. You wouldn’t be able to create a sub-project if you create a parent project first, because all Pipenv commands will see the parent project, and refuse to create a new one.

The only reasonable way to work around this is to create the new Pipfile manually (e.g. `touch Pipfile`).  This is however still hackish, and not at all obvious unless you are knowledgable about Pipenv’s internals. With Pipenv encouraging user to let it handle Pipfile edits entirely (with `pipenv install/uninstall [package]` commands), it would also confuse users if it breaks consistency here.

The proposal is to provide an obvious way for user to initialise a virtual environment with a Pipfile created in the current directory, similar to `npm init`. This does not change the current initialisation behaviour, but only provide an additional way to initialise not only the virtual environment, but the Pipfile explicitly, right here. There are several possible ways to implement this:

* An environment variable. This is probably not a good idea, since this needs to be obvious to be useful. Environment variabls are not obvious at all, at least to me. It would also conflict with `PIPENV_PIPFILE`.
* An option to `pipenv`, e.g. `pipenv --python=... --project=.`. This is not as obvious as a top-level subcommand, but is still reasonably visible, and can be combined with other subcommands to perform tasks. It would still conflict with `PIPENV_PIPFILE`. What would `PIPENV_PIPFILE=/path/a/Pipfile pipenv --python=... --project=/path/b` even mean?
* An extra sub-command, *a la* `npm init`. This is the most obvious solution, but given the history of reluctancy to adding new commands, I can understand this may not be the preferred way to go. I do prefer this the most, and it is the only option that does not conflict with `PIPENV_PIPFILE`. It can simply throw an error if a Pipfile already exists at the target location, and `PIPENV_PIPFILE` errors if the specifies location does not exist.

issue_title: pipenv basic tutorial should link to valid values of python_version
issue_body:
Reading [the basic usage tutorial](https://docs.pipenv.org/basics/) through for the first time mentions `python_version`, but doesn't link to what the acceptable values are for that option.

Concretely, since my project uses PyPy, I put `pypy` in there, which appears to "work" in the sense that the virtualenv was created using PyPy when then running `pipenv install`, but I had to guess whether that was actually a thing.

issue_title: There is no way to set per-environment indexes
issue_body:
We have different pip mirror urls (both public and private) depending on environments. Our private mirror is password protected. It would be nice to be able to provide global source configuration, without having to commit urls into all of the repositories, and also avoid having to store passwords in the repo (or enter them every time you are updating dependencies).

Pip does that via `.pip/pip.conf` file, where you can have `[global]` section with your per-environment configuration which makes work that Ops have to do a lot easier.

This also would be useful in environments where one wants to prevent global pypi from being used (to avoid depending on resources external to the datacenter in the build process), without modifying code being built/installed.

issue_title: auditwheel show also works on conda packages
issue_body:
Not a bug report, rather a note on a nice feature.  I found that auditwheel can also be used to audit a conda package for external shared libraries.  I have not tested if any of the other sub-commands work.  Thanks for this tool, it is really helpful.

```

GLIBCXX_3.4.19.

This constrains the platform tag to "linux_x86_64". In order to
achieve a more compatible tag, you would to recompile a new wheel from
source on a system with earlier versions of these libraries, such as

issue_title: Complete implementation of multipart put
Review documentation across all functions
Handling optional "1" (e.g. "(1) day ago") for some languages
issue_body:
In EN we say "1 day ago", not "day ago".  
However in some other languages the "1" here may be optional.  
E.g. in CS one may say "před týden" for "1 week ago" (před->ago, týden->week).

The current version of dateparser (130c0e8) can handle "před 1 týden" but not "před týden".

issue_title: Importing dateparser taking a significant time
Properly parsing 'Wednesday'
issue_body:
Hi,

I've been using this great library for a while but I'm running into an issue with parsing relative dates like "wednesday". This returns the past week's wednesday (if today is Sunday (3/4/18), it'll return (2/28/18). I know there's a setting for `PREFER_DATES_FROM: future` but the problem with that is if I have that turned on, wednesday parses correctly to 03/07/18. However,  if we then try to parse 03/07/18 it'll become 03-07-2**1**18 (due to the line here). What's the best solution to account for parsing both wednesday & 03/07/18 correctly?

issue_title: testtools matching utility for requests
issue_body:
So... I wrote this thing, not sure where to put it:
```python
class RequestSequence(treq_RequestSequence):
    @contextmanager
    def consume(self, sync_failure_reporter):
        yield
        if not self.consumed():
            sync_failure_reporter("\n".join(
                ["Not all expected requests were made.  Still expecting:"] +
                ["- {0!r})".format(e) for e, _ in self._sequence]))

    def __call__(self, method, url, params, headers, data):
        """
        :return: the next response in the sequence, provided that the
            parameters match the next in the sequence.
        """
        req = (method, url, params, headers, data)
        if len(self._sequence) == 0:
            self._async_reporter(
                None, Never(),
                "No more requests expected, but request {0!r} made.".format(
                    req))
            return (500, {}, "StubbingError")
        matcher, response = self._sequence[0]
        self._async_reporter(req, matcher)
        self._sequence = self._sequence[1:]
        return response
```

You use it by passing in `self.expectThat` as the "async reporter" (I kinda overloaded this to mean something else), and by passing in a matcher instead of a 5-tuple to match against the request.

You might construct the matcher something like this:
```python
MatchesListwise([
    Equals(b'POST'),
    Equals(u'https://example.org/'),
    ContainsDict({b'foo': Equals(b'bar')}),
    Always(),
    Equals(b'')])
```

Because you have the full power of testtools matchers, you don't need to resort to tricks like `HasHeaders` or `mock.ANY` to do something other than an exact equality match.

Obviously this is probably only of interest to people using testtools, though.

issue_title: nbsp character breaks json conversion
issue_body:
Apparently when having nbsp chars as separator, causes js-yaml to parse the document incorrectly.

For example: (nbsp character is between `region: Ceredigion`

```yaml
{ city: Aberaeron, region: Ceredigion }
```
```yaml
{ city: Aberaeron, region:☠Ceredigion } #visual representation
```

```json
{
  "city": "Aberaeron",
  "region: Ceredigion": null
},
```

Apparently when converting with [yamljs](https://github.com/jeremyfa/yaml.js) the document is parsed correctly independently of the `nbsp` char, generating:

```json
{
  "city": "Aberaeron",
  "region": "Ceredigion"
},
```

issue_title: Reply to the Cursor-Position-Report sequence
issue_body:
pyte should respond to the "Cursor Position Report" sequence:

- http://vt100.net/docs/vt510-rm/CPR.html

An application prints capability `u6`, which terminal emulator discovers and replies in format of capability `u7`. For xterm, this is `'\x1b[6n'` and `'\x1b[%i%d;%dR'`, respectively. I implemented the "client side" of this in blessed, which contains many details about this sequence.

I intend to use this functionality and will contribute it if necessary as needed downstream for test case of https://github.com/jquast/blessed/issues/94

issue_title: Remove param={} from all the methods
issue_body:
Hi there,

The issue with [having mutable default arguments](https://github.com/segmentio/analytics-python/blob/master/analytics/client.py#L44-L45) is [that](http://docs.python-guide.org/en/latest/writing/gotchas/#mutable-default-arguments). Usually it can be replaced by `param = param or {}` or `param = param or []`.

Max.

issue_title: Latest Stripe (1.34.0) won't JSON encode in Python 2.7.9
issue_body:
As far as I can tell, it's a bug (?) in the [C version of the JSON encoder](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/2.7/Modules/_json.c) in the Python standard library. Here are the steps to reproduce:

```pythonshell
>>> import stripe
>>> stripe.api_key = 'sk_test_XXXXXXXX'
>>> import json
>>> evt = stripe.Event.retrieve('evt_XXXXXXXXX')
>>> result = json.dumps(evt)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-5-df22ddbe60b6> in <module>()
----> 1 result = json.dumps(evt)

/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.9/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/__init__.pyc in dumps(obj, skipkeys, ensure_ascii, check_circular, allow_nan, cls, indent, separators, encoding, default, sort_keys, **kw)
    241         cls is None and indent is None and separators is None and
    242         encoding == 'utf-8' and default is None and not sort_keys and not kw):
--> 243         return _default_encoder.encode(obj)
    244     if cls is None:
    245         cls = JSONEncoder

/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.9/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.pyc in encode(self, o)
    205         # exceptions aren't as detailed.  The list call should be roughly
    206         # equivalent to the PySequence_Fast that ''.join() would do.
--> 207         chunks = self.iterencode(o, _one_shot=True)
    208         if not isinstance(chunks, (list, tuple)):
    209             chunks = list(chunks)

/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.9/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.pyc in iterencode(self, o, _one_shot)
    268                 self.key_separator, self.item_separator, self.sort_keys,
    269                 self.skipkeys, _one_shot)
--> 270         return _iterencode(o, 0)
    271
    272 def _make_iterencode(markers, _default, _encoder, _indent, _floatstr,

TypeError: keys must be a string
```

Forcing the Python version of the encoder to run does not trigger this ([the code that determines which encoder is run is here](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/2.7/Lib/json/encoder.py#L259-L269)):

```
>>> result = json.dumps(evt, indent=4)
>>> type(result)
str
```

Manually running `.items()` on varying levels of the `Event` object structure doesn't reveal anything useful (they keys are all `str` or `unicode`). Passing `skipkeys=True` to the encoder prevents the exception, but the encoding stops at this depth: `evt['data']['object']['lines']` (which I'll note is a `ListObject` which I have specifically had issues with on the `json` encoder in the past -- which I got around by passing `list_object.data` to the `json` encoder rather than `list_object`).

I will also note that this issue is _not_ present in Python 3.x:

```
$ ipython3
Python 3.3.5 (default, Mar 13 2014, 10:39:48)
Type "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.

IPython 1.2.1 -- An enhanced Interactive Python.
?         -> Introduction and overview of IPython's features.
%quickref -> Quick reference.
help      -> Python's own help system.
object?   -> Details about 'object', use 'object??' for extra details.

In [1]: import stripe

In [2]: stripe.api_key = 'sk_test_XXXXXXXXX'

In [3]: import json

In [4]: evt = stripe.Event.retrieve('evt_XXXXXXXXX')

In [5]: result = json.dumps(evt)

In [6]: 
```

I started experiencing this issue when upgrading from `1.19.1` to `1.33.0` (also present in `1.34.0`). I would normally work around something like this by specifying a function to `defaults` keyword argument in the json encoder (specifying a work-around for the `ListObject` class), but since everything in the `stripe` library inherits from `StripeObject` which is a subclass of `dict`, the encoder never tosses a `ValueError`, and therefore never attempts to run the `defaults` function (instead trying to encode it internally as a `dict`).

issue_title: imsave() produces incorrect output for 3D array with width or height 1
issue_body:
To reproduce this bug, simply construct a 3D array with width or height 1 and use skimage.io.imsave(). For instance, we could save a 1x1 color image:

    >>> a=numpy.random.random((1,1,3))
    >>> skimage.io.imsave('a.png', a)
    ...
      File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/skimage/io/_plugins/pil_plugin.py", line 258, in imsave
        raise ValueError("Invalid shape for image array: %s" % arr.shape)
    ValueError: Invalid shape for image array: 3

The correct result would be to save a 1x1 PNG file.

Or we could save a width 1, height 3 color image:

    >>> a=numpy.random.random((3,1,3))
    >>> skimage.io.imsave('a.png', a)
    >>> skimage.io.imread('a.png').shape
    (3, 3)

This saves a 3x3 greyscale image instead!

The culprit is line 258 of skimage/io/_plugins/pil_plugin.py:

    arr = np.asanyarray(arr).squeeze()

The squeeze function removes all size one dimensions, which makes it impossible to save images with width or height one.

The exact fix depends on the desired semantics.

One conjectures that the squeeze() function is currently being used for convenience if one wants to save malformed slices from a high dimensional array. In general though, for this use case, it will be impossible to determine which dimensions correspond to actual height and width, and which correspond to size one slices that should be squeezed away. One possible resolution to this ambiguity is to assume that only trailing dimensions are "auto-squeezed" if the dimensionality is too large. That is, replace the above line with:

    arr = np.asanyarray(arr)
    if arr.ndim > 3:
      remaining_shape = arr.shape[3:]
      if remaining_shape == (1,)*len(remaining_shape):
        arr = arr.reshape(arr.shape[:3])
      else: 
        raise ValueError('malformed image shape: image array is more than 3 dims and trailing dimensions are not all one so they cannot be auto-squeezed')

issue_title: Potentially running too many iterations in ransac
issue_body:
in skimage/measure/fit.py
managing the number of iterations is done with max_trials. This is compared to _dynamic_max_trials which is computed using the largest inlier group found so far. However, this comparison is done only in cases when sample_inlier_num is larger than the biggest group. In cases when a large group is found early (but not large enough to end) the best_inlier_num is updated but the max_trials is not updated and will only be checked next time a larger group is found.

the lines (926) :

            if (
                best_inlier_num >= stop_sample_num
                or best_inlier_residuals_sum <= stop_residuals_sum
                or num_trials
                    >= _dynamic_max_trials(best_inlier_num, num_samples,
                                           min_samples, stop_probability)
            ):
                break
should be something like

             max_trials = min(max_trials, _dynamic_max_trials(best_inlier_num, num_samples,
                                           min_samples, stop_probability))
            if (
                best_inlier_num >= stop_sample_num
                or best_inlier_residuals_sum <= stop_residuals_sum
                or num_trials > max_trials
            ):
                break

issue_title: temporal_memory: reset() method needs to reset matching cells and matching segments too.
issue_body:
The reset() method currently looks like this:

``` Python

def reset(self):
    """
    Indicates the start of a new sequence. Resets sequence state of the TM.
    """
    self.activeCells = set()
    self.predictiveCells = set()
    self.activeSegments = set()
    self.winnerCells = set()
```

It should be changed to include **"matchingCells"** and **"matchingSegments"** like this:

``` Python
def reset(self):
    """
    Indicates the start of a new sequence. Resets sequence state of the TM.
    """
    self.activeCells = set()
    self.predictiveCells = set()
    self.activeSegments = set()
    self.winnerCells = set()
    self.matchingCells = set()
    self.matchingSegments = set()
```

The reason for this is that they are passed into both the "activateCorrectlyPredictiveCells()" and "learnOnSegments" methods which process those lists and update state based on that processing.

Seen here in their respective order:

temporal_memory.py line 267
``` Python
if self.predictedSegmentDecrement > 0:
      for cell in **prevMatchingCells**:            <-----
        column = self.columnForCell(cell)

        if column not in activeColumns:
          predictedInactiveCells.add(cell)
```

temporal_memory.py line 392
``` Python
if self.predictedSegmentDecrement > 0:
      for segment in **prevMatchingSegments**:    <-----
        isPredictedInactiveCell = connections.cellForSegment(segment) in predictedInactiveCells
        activeSynapses = self.activeSynapsesForSegment(
          segment, prevActiveCells, connections)

        if isPredictedInactiveCell:
          self.adaptSegment(segment, activeSynapses, connections,
                            -self.predictedSegmentDecrement,
                            0.0)
```

issue_title: [Question] Working with autocomplete and foreign languages
issue_body:
I've implemented the cities-light + autocomplete in English and everything is working well. Now I'm trying to do 2 things:

1. display names in foreign languages. I've read somewhere that I could use django-modeltranslation to help that out, so I will explore that further;

2. enable my users to search/autocomplete when they type in foreign characters (in particular, simplified Chinese). I've been digging around, experimenting with different approaches. For example, I edit/modify fields 'name', 'display_name', 'search_name', etc , and it seems that (for City) the autocomplete is searching against the 'name' column. In particular, when I change the 'name' field for Shanghai to '上海' (Chinese for Shanghai), and I type in "上" on the web form, the autocomplete returns "Shanghai, Shanghai, China" (the display name) as expected. 

However, I want the English searching/autocompleting to keep working as before. So, I tried changing the 'name' column to '上海Shanghai' / '上海 Shanghai' / 
'上海 shanghai' ,etc. , but regardless of the how I put the second part, the English autocomplete does not work (if I type "shang", Shanghai does not get returned). 

So I continued experimenting. If I make the 'name' field something like  'Shanghai上海' (English first, Chinese afterwards), the English autocomplete works, but the Chinese one stops working. It feels almost like it's searching with a 'begins_with" filter ... I'm sure it's not, but I'm not sure what's happening...

In summary, I would appreciate very much any advice on how to proceed with the two challenges I face: 1. displaying cities/countries in different languages (is modeltranslation the way to go?); and 2. Kindly walk me through what autocomplete is searching against here, and what I should do to enable the autocomplete for cities in both Chinese and English.

Thanks!

issue_title: RFC: deposit: multiuser submission
issue_body:
**Problem:**

Currently, deposit module is dependent on having a current user, and the data model is tied to a user id.

This prevents both:

- Guest submissions in the deposit module (~~needed by Inspire~~).
- Multiple users access to a single deposition (needed by Zenodo).

**Proposal:**

- Remove user id property from deposition model.
- Add model for controlling access to deposition (e.g. many-to-many relationship between a depositions and (users ~~or access tokens~~)).
- ~~Allow access to a deposition based on an access token (to support guest submissions). This allow long running guest submissions, and sharing a guest submission. Likely the access token should expire and deposition cleaned be up after a while.~~

Co-authored-by: @lnielsen-cern, @kneczaj @pedrogaudencio @giokokos

The issue with this can be:

1) Recreating records in ES from DB. As there is no JSON, the record will not be sent to ES and thus will not be searchable. Might be useful to keep deleted records in ES to do some searches on them.

2) In a future record editor, where we load records from DB, extra logic will have to be added in order to fetch the record, e.g from the last non-empty history entry. Instead if the record stayed in DB the record would be opened normally and could be un-deleted.

Would it be possible to make this configurable or leave the records still in the DB when soft deleting?

issue_title: RFC merge RECORDS_REST_SORT_OPTIONS inside RECORDS_REST_ENDPOINTS
issue_body:
**Problem**

Currently we have a special configuration called `RECORDS_REST_SORT_OPTIONS` that specifies the sort configuration for each `index`.

 1. It can be error prone because the user have to specify the index name inside `RECORDS_REST_ENDPOINTS` and, in the same time, inside `RECORDS_REST_SORT_OPTIONS`.

 2. In addition, It is not intuitive because the convention for the dictionary key is different from `RECORDS_REST_ENDPOINTS` (where as `key` is used the `pid_type`).

 3. Inside [sorter.py](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-rest/blob/master/invenio_records_rest/sorter.py#L140-L148) we set the sort options to the `search_class`. So, why we don't do it directly?

**Proposal**
Can we directly specify the default sort configuration inside the [search class](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-rest/blob/master/invenio_records_rest/config.py#L44) and remove `RECORDS_REST_SORT_OPTIONS`?

----------------------------------------------------
cc @lnielsen @tiborsimko @jirikuncar 
see https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-rest/issues/99

issue_title: Pure-Python Wrapper objects break `object.__getattribute__` in methods of the wrapped object
issue_body:
Certain "base" classes make direct use of `object.__getattribute__` in their method implementations. (i.e., *outside* of their own `__getattribute__` implementation). They may do this for performance reasons, or to simplify their implementation of `__getattribute__`.  One prominent example is `persistent.Persistent`, but there are undoubtedly others.

If classes like this are wrapped with a pure-Python acquisition wrapper, those methods fail, because the `self` they have is not the `self` they expect (it's the wrapper), and directly calling `object.__getattribute__` bypasses the wrapper's `__getattribute__` implementation.

Here's an example test that fails with pure-Python, but works under the C implementation:

```python
    def test_object_getattribute_in_rebound_method(self):

        class Persistent(object):
            __slots__ = ('__flags')
            def __init__(self):
                self.__flags = 42

            def get_flags(self):
                return object.__getattribute__(self, '_Persistent__flags')

        wrapped = Persistent()
        wrapper = Acquisition.ImplicitAcquisitionWrapper(wrapped, None)

        self.assertEqual(wrapped.get_flags(), wrapper.get_flags())
```

It fails like so:
```
  File "/Acquisition/tests.py", line 3171, in test_object_getattribute_in_rebound_method

issue_title: "Transaction.abort" should abort the resources in the inverse order then "commit" commits the resources
issue_body:
The order in which resources are committed during a commit can be important. For this reason, this order can be influenced by defining a method `sortKey` on the resources. For `abort`, the resources are aborted in registration order - independent of `sortKey`.

However, the order in which resources are aborted can be important as well. In my case, a Plone portal should be created. During the operation, a ZODB connection and various `collective.indexing.transactions.QueueTM` objects register with the transaction (in this order). An error during the portal creation causes a `transaction.abort()` which fails, because the connection abort ghostifies the portal's component registry and the `collective.indexing.transactions.QueueTM` abort depends on it.

It would be good to have control not only over the `commit` order but also over the `abort` order of resources. I assume that aborting resources in the inverse order of committing them would most likely give acceptable results.

issue_title: Test that makes no sense?
issue_body:
When running the tests against current master under Python 2.7 using the standard `bin/test` script they all complete successfully. However, running them via `tox` produces non-deterministic errors. Example:

```python
Failure in test test_runAllExportSteps_empty (Products.GenericSetup.tests.test_tool.SetupToolTests)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.13/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/unittest/case.py", line 329, in run
    testMethod()
  File "/Users/jens/src/Products.GenericSetup/Products/GenericSetup/tests/test_tool.py", line 890, in test_runAllExportSteps_empty
    self.assertEqual(result['steps'][1], 'step_registries')
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.13/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/unittest/case.py", line 513, in assertEqual
    assertion_func(first, second, msg=msg)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.13/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/unittest/case.py", line 506, in _baseAssertEqual
    raise self.failureException(msg)
AssertionError: u'toolset' != 'step_registries'
```

The test code (see https://github.com/zopefoundation/Products.GenericSetup/blob/master/Products/GenericSetup/tests/test_tool.py#L882) appears to assume that `result` from a call to `tool.runAllExportSteps()` has a fixed order of steps. But that makes no sense when I look at how the result is put together. In `listExportSteps` the steps are combined from two `set` instances, which obviously don't have a sorting order.

Is the assumption of a fixed order wrong or is the implementation wrong which disregards any order?

issue_title: Questions about /password/reset/confirm
issue_body:
I've done some testing, and I'm puzzled about the /password/reset/confirm endpoint. I'm probably missing something obvious...

The password-reset email will contain a link of the form /password/reset/confirm/{id}/{token}. Your documentation says this is a POST endpoint. yet the browser will issue a GET when the user clicks on the link. How does this work?

The URLconf has the regex r'^password/reset/confirm$', yet the link will be /password/reset/confirm/{id}/{token} . The regex includes the EOL match, so I don't see how this can ever match the URL.

In the ActionViewMixin, it appears that request.DATA expects the uid and token to have been sent in via a query string. It also seems to expect the new_password to be present, otherwise self.get_serializer() returns False.  Is this correct?

Hi, I've just started to use your lib in my setup. It fits pretty well since I can customise the endpoints I want to use. However when it comes to generating the confirmation url on prod I'm afraid I'll always get a insecure url generated by djoser due the following check coupled with my setup.

```python
return cls(
            from_email=from_email,
            user=user or request.user,
            domain=django_settings.DJOSER.get('DOMAIN') or site.domain,
            site_name=django_settings.DJOSER.get('SITE_NAME') or site.name,
            protocol='https' if request.is_secure() else 'http',
            **context
        )
```

class CustomPasswordResetView(PasswordResetView):

Do you think this is a good approach?

issue_title: Compilation error due to macro BROTLI_ALLOC in enc/memory.h
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm compiling ngx_brotli, with v0.6.0 brotli release (but the macro is the same in current master branch), and I had to fix an issue with this macro:
```
../ngx_brotli_test/deps/brotli/enc/./block_splitter_inc.h:403:61: error: ‘*’ in boolean context, suggest ‘&&’ instead [-Werror=int-in-bool-context]
     double* insert_cost = BROTLI_ALLOC(m, double, data_size * num_histograms);
../ngx_brotli_test/deps/brotli/enc/././memory.h:43:5: note: in definition of macro ‘BROTLI_ALLOC’
   ((N) ? ((T*)BrotliAllocate((M), (N) * sizeof(T))) : NULL)
     ^
```
Just replace the macro like this:
```c
#define BROTLI_ALLOC(M, T, N)                               \
  ((N) > 0 ? ((T*)BrotliAllocate((M), (N) * sizeof(T))) : NULL)
```
(test if (N) is strictly greater that zero).

I believe this is the fix, it compiled fine after changing this (haven't tried nginx with ngx_brotli after that, what is my concern, I will do so...).

I'm using GCC 7.1.1 (as available in ArchLinux repos); nginx does **not** add a parameter like `-std` to commands.

```
cc -c -pipe  -O -W -Wall -Wpointer-arith -Wno-unused-parameter -Werror -g -fPIE -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2 -fstack-check -fstack-protector-strong -Wno-deprecated-declarations  -I src/core -I src/event -I src/event/modules -I src/os/unix -I objs -I src/http -I src/http/modules -I ../ngx_brotli_test/deps/brotli/include \
	-o objs/addon/enc/block_splitter.o \
	../ngx_brotli_test/deps/brotli/enc/block_splitter.c
```

ngx_brotli repo actually uses the commit 222564a95d9ab58865a096b8d9f7324ea5f2e03e for brotli, if there is any security concern about this.

If you agree to, I can try to submit a PR (on both repos, in ngx_brotli it's just updating the list of files in enc) so I can have my name somewhere in the history :)

TypeError: unhashable type: 'set' due to commit 93dd4ab82fd611812392d760f577b2fe71f80f19
issue_body:
I've commented here:
https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/commit/93dd4ab82fd611812392d760f577b2fe71f80f19#commitcomment-18527777

Effectively, a stray comma has created a tuple out of what should've been just a set.

A set consuming a tuple of a set is unhashable, whereas the previous code had lacked a comma that lead to a set consuming a set (a safe operation).

Deleting that comma will fix the issue.

Raven-python 5.24.0 break for Flask applications, which is rather unfortunate as I now have to force the 5.23.X series for my deployments.

issue_title: Logging integration with multiple threads
issue_body:
I'm trying to use `raven.handlers.logging.SentryHandler` to capture errors and exceptions in my application, but I'm getting some strange behaviour that I'm hoping I can get a little help understanding. Potentially this is related to #806 (which @mitsuhiko was looking at), but seeing this behaviour without multiprocessing involved felt like it warranted a new ticket.

MWE:

```
import raven
import logging
from raven.handlers.logging import SentryHandler
from time import sleep
from threading import Thread

logger = logging.getLogger('foo')
logger.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
other_logger = logging.getLogger('bar')
other_logger.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)

client = raven.Client()
sentryLogHandler = SentryHandler(client, level="ERROR")
logger.addHandler(sentryLogHandler)
other_logger.addHandler(sentryLogHandler)

sh = logging.StreamHandler()
logger.addHandler(sh)
other_logger.addHandler(sh)

logger.debug("1")
logger.info("2")
other_logger.info("3")

def foo():
    other_logger.info("4")
    logger.critical("5")
t = Thread(target=foo)
t.start()
sleep(0.1)

logger.critical("6")
```

Output in the console (via `StreamHandler`) is as expected:

```
1
2
3
4
5
6
```

However, in Sentry:

- The event for `6` is missing the breadcrumbs for `4` and `5` (from the other thread), and
- The event for `5` has no breadcrumbs at all, not even the `4` one from within the same thread.

Any idea what's going on here?

issue_title: "NameError: name 'self' is not defined" (RDFa parser, with TIME element containing another element)
issue_body: Given an HTML structure like the following: 2016-01-01 the RDFa parser will abort with an unhandled NameError exception because the `_get_literal()` function body references `self` but we are not in an OO context at the time. I'm working on a fix, with a unit test.

On forums like Stackoverflow one finds disagreement about whether running `pip` with `sudo` is perfectly fine, or a terrible idea. issue_title: speed-up handling of datasets with >> 100 columns
issue_body:
```
import datatable as dt
import time

class MyLogger:
    def __init__(self):
        self.messages = ""
    def info(self, msg):
        self.messages += msg + '\n'

ml = MyLogger()
dt.options.core_logger = ml

t0=time.time()
a = dt.open("train.bin")['afb6b8217']
print("%.5g" % (time.time()-t0))

print(ml.messages)
```

[train.zip](https://github.com/h2oai/datatable/files/2128904/train.zip)


This takes most of the time in core.datatable_load() (0.01s) and dt.Frame (0.02s) for a total of 0.03s.  DAI  does this *per column*, but then cumulates this overhead because these operations have *all column* operations.

```
diff --git a/datatable/nff.py b/datatable/nff.py                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             
index 4ee3862..400255b 100644

+++ b/datatable/nff.py                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       
@@ -12,7 +12,7 @@ from datatable.lib import core                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             
 # from datatable.fread import Frame                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         
 # from datatable.fread import fread                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         
 from datatable.utils.typechecks import typed, TValueError, dtwarn                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
-                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
+import time                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
 _builtin_open = open                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             
 def _stringify(x):                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
@@ -73,6 +73,7 @@ def save(self, dest, _strategy="auto"):                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             
 @typed(path=str)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
 def open(path):                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             
+    t0a = time.time()
     path = os.path.expanduser(path)
     if not os.path.exists(path):
         msg = "Path %s does not exist" % path
@@ -81,10 +82,12 @@ def open(path):
         raise ValueError(msg)
     if not os.path.isdir(path):
         raise ValueError("%s is not a directory" % path)
+    t0b = time.time()
 
     nff_version = None
     nrows = 0
     metafile = os.path.join(path, "_meta.nff")
+    t0c = time.time()
     with _builtin_open(metafile, encoding="utf-8") as inp:
         info = []
         for line in inp:
@@ -109,13 +112,20 @@ def open(path):
         else:
             raise ValueError("Unknown NFF format: %s" % info[0])
 
+    t0d = time.time()
     coltypes = [dt.stype.str32] * 4
     if nff_version > 1:
         coltypes += [None] * 2
+    t0e = time.time()
     f0 = dt.fread(metafile, sep=",", columns=coltypes)
+    t0f = time.time()
     f1 = f0(select=["filename", "stype", "meta"])
     colnames = f0["colname"].topython()[0]
+    t0g = time.time()
     _dt = core.datatable_load(f1.internal, nrows, path)
+    t0h = time.time()
     df = dt.Frame(_dt, names=colnames)
+    t0i = time.time()
     assert df.nrows == nrows, "Wrong number of rows read: %d" % df.nrows
+    print("%.5g %.5g %.5g %.5g %.5g %.5g %.5g %.5g" % (t0b-t0a, t0c-t0b, t0d-t0c, t0e-t0d, t0f-t0e, t0g-t0f, t0h-t0g, t0i-t0h))
     return df

```

issue_title: Using results of Poisson CCD Simulator to simulate BF effect
issue_body: Craig Lage at UC Davis has developed a Poisson equation solver that computes the distortion of the pixel edges as the electrons get collected in the CCD pixels. In the DESC Hack day today, we made some progress towards integrating his code (in C++) with the C++ layer of the GalSim. We identified  that the natural way to include this functionality would be by tweaking the photon-shooting method inside the `drawImage` routine.  I will push the code after some cleanup, but this is not completely done yet.

issue_title: neurom.iter_neurites should accept lists of trees
issue_body: Currently `neurom.iter_neurites` can deal with individual neurite trees, neurons and neuron populations. It should be extended to deal with iterables of of neurite trees (e.g. lists or tuples.)

issue_title: re-enable bifurcating soma in special case
issue_body:
It seems issue #475 was over-restrictive, making the commonly used SWC "3 point soma" structure illegal. In this 3-point structure, the second and third soma points are children of the first. This means the first point is a bifurcating soma point. Allow this when

* The soma contains exactly three points
* The first (soma) point is the parent of the second and third soma points

issue_title: Allow a cluster member to bootstrap in the absence of the leader
issue_body:
Previously, we could only use pg_basebackup to bootstrap the new replica, which obviously required a running master. Currently, we also have a possibility to use WAL-E, as well as user-supplied replica creation methods that don't require a running master, since the data is stored on S3 or any other external storage. We need to change the Patroni behavior to bootstrap even if the leader is not present, in case the replica creation method allows it. Since there is no way we can auto detect the ability of the replica creation method to create replicas without the running master, we should add a parameter to the method configuration, indicating that.

The result is that we can recover from stupid errors, like the case of someone just shutting down all Patroni instances at once.

issue_title: entity
issue_labels: [{'id': 398939782, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/springload/draftjs_exporter/labels/enhancement', 'name': 'enhancement', 'color': '84b6eb', 'default': True}, {'id': 815973805, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/springload/draftjs_exporter/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': '7057ff', 'default': True}, {'id': 398939783, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/springload/draftjs_exporter/labels/help%20wanted', 'name': 'help wanted', 'color': '128A0C', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
Follow-up to #87. At the moment, entity components are only given a small amount of data about an entity for rendering: https://github.com/springload/draftjs_exporter/blob/764d377659f6c6b48be826c7a7d98cfdbc152015/draftjs_exporter/entity_state.py#L48-L58

There are a few more problems here:

- The shape of the props is different than how this is stored in the Draft.js `entityMap`.
- The entity `data` is given as the top-level props, which makes it impossible to add extra props without risking overwriting the entity data (say if the entity has a field called `type` that does something different from the entity type).

We should refactor this to something like:

```python
props = {}
props['entity'] = entity_details
props['entity']['key'] = key
props['block'] = block
props['blocks'] = blocks
```

This way, it's easy to add arbitrary props without risking overwrites. The components get full access to the entity data, mutability, type, and key. And to the current block and blocks list, like styles, decorators, blocks.

Compared to the changes introduced in #90, this would be a **breaking change**, so should be considered carefully. Users of the exporter will have to rewrite their renderers slightly to use the new props shape.

issue_title: Setting maxResults=None does not fetch all sprints
issue_body:
If there are more than 50 sprints, only the first 50 are returned when maxResults is a falsey value.

```
client.sprints(1, maxResults=None, state='closed')
```

In _fetch_results it sets `total = resource.get('total', 1)`. The sprints api does not return a 'total' in the resource, so it gets set to 1.

Then inside the `if not maxResults` section it checks whether `total is None or page_start < total` which evaluates to `1 is None or 50 < 1` and doesn't enter the while loop.

Given that the code is already written to handle `total is None`, I believe the correct fix is to change the assignment of total to default to None: `total = resource.get('total')`

issue_title: Allow usage of bytearray objects (or other raw buffers) for secure password passing
issue_body:
Python offers a memoryview or buffer interface to raw-bytes, e.g. to bytearrays, mmap or custom objects implementing the buffer interface. PyString/PyBytes objects are inherently dangerous for passing password data, as those cannot be zeroed reliably after use. But buffer objects can be zeroed, as they are mutable string like structures.

So using `z#` instead of the `z` modifier for `PyArg_ParseTupleAndKeywords` would naturally allow using those more secure ways to pass password data to winkerberos.

issue_title: Improve sphinx documentation builds
issue_body:
Currently the "doc" command for setup runs sphinx-build on a subprocess. This is problematic for a few reasons:

- On Windows it is highly unlikely that sphinx-build will be on the users path, unless the build is run from a virtualenv.
- Even if sphinx-build is on the user's path, it may use a different interpreter than the interpreter used to run "setup.py doc". For example, running "C:\Python34\python.exe setup.py doc", but sphinx-build is "C:\Python27\Scripts\sphinx-build.exe"

We should import sphinx and call sphinx.main directly to avoid both of these problems.

issue_title: Don't use stdout for communication between executor and its subprocess
issue_body:
Currently, [executor communicates with its subprocess via reading subprocess' stdout and parsing JSON (and other specially formatted) lines from it](https://github.com/genialis/resolwe/blob/master/resolwe/flow/executors/__init__.py#L178-L253).

This can cause many subtle problems, like the out encountered in [#220](https://github.com/genialis/resolwe/pull/220#issuecomment-302466959), where the progress of the `curl` command written to the standard output interleaved with the JSON outputted by the subsequent `echo` command. The executor failed to parse JSON from a standard output line like:
```
  0     0    0     0    0     0      0      0 --:--:-- --:--:-- --:--:--     0{"collection-list": [{"slug":"collection-foo","name":"collection-foo","description":"","settings":{},"descriptor_schema":null,"descriptor":{},"data":[50],"contributor":{"id":117,"username":"contributor","first_name":"","last_name":""},"id":31,"created":"2017-03-24T03:37:17.417596","modified":"2017-03-24T03:37:17.417634","descriptor_dirty":false,"permissions":[{"permissions":["view"],"type":"public"}]}]}
```
which caused the process to not work as expected.

We should implement a more robust solution for communication between executor and its subprocess.
