issue_title: Reading Argentinian H5 files and saving in CfRadial
issue_body:
Hi everyone,

this is not an actual issue of PyART but an enhancement proposal separated in two parts:

I updated the sinarame_h5 reader to match the odim_h5 reader. It's mostly the same but there are some 'unique' features from the h5 files. I will soon submit a PR with this stuff.

The second thing is something @swnesbitt tried to pull in #480. Writing cfradial from the h5 files is something I found really useful and I know some people who can use this as well.

This particular issue I found was on the way the original bufr files are created:  the variables are in separated files, they have different gate bins, strategies applied, etc. It's a huge mess to work with this data as is. I know this is a _very_ specific case, but I came up with a script (adapting something Steve has done) that saves all the variables and their metadata in one cfradial file. Should this be added as another function in the `sinarame_h5` file? Or, as discussed in the PR, it will fit better as an example or in the wiki?

I'm polishing that script (because it's in spanish right now) and I'll upload it a gist so you have a better idea of what I'm talking about. In the meantime let me know what you think of this addition.

We have a number of sporadic system test failures (#1085, #1089, #1100, #1104) due to "eventual consistency" in the tested APIs.  We have adopted a set of ad-hoc (and obviously not 100% reliable) strategies for working around the delays involved.

issue_title: datastore: Query does not allow inequality filter on __key__ property.
issue_body:
Datastore query raises a ValueError for inequality(non-equality to be correct) operators on __key__ property. 
https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/master/gcloud/datastore/query.py#L223

According to Google Cloud Datastore documentation inquality operators on __key__ property are supported, and should not raise exception.
https://cloud.google.com/datastore/docs/concepts/queries#Datastore_Key_filters

It is a critical part of our data-model.

issue_title: DISCUSSION: Does a Transaction need to be bound to a Connection and vice versa
issue_body:
It seems we could have `connection=None` as an optional / implicit argument in the `Transaction` methods `add_auto_id_entity()`, `begin()`, `commit()` and `rollback()`.

On `Connection`, the current transaction is used to alter the behavior of the methods, e.g. from [`commit()`][1]
```python
        if self.transaction():
            request.mode = datastore_pb.CommitRequest.TRANSACTIONAL
            request.transaction = self.transaction().id()
        else:
            request.mode = datastore_pb.CommitRequest.NON_TRANSACTIONAL
```

It seems instead this separation would benefit by having `Transaction` subclass `Connection` and adding the "extra" transaction parts when needed.

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/8faba35a266cd6280f125adf6035f902a07a26e4/gcloud/datastore/connection.py#L359-L363

issue_title: Sharding with range filters results in poor workload distribution
issue_body:
This is a somewhat old issue that I doesn't seem to have gotten much attention. I found a Google Groups post that articulates the problem and proposes a fix for it here: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/app-engine-pipeline-api/O_5aaoNsS04

Problems summary:
Presently equality[sic] filters are ignored when choosing key range split points using the __scatter__ property. The resulting split points are appropriate for the entire population of entities of a given kind, but not necessarily for the sub-population matching the equality filter(s). This can result in a very uneven distribution of work among shards.

I thought I'd re-file it here in case that group is no longer monitored.
The fix seems simple enough and I'm happy to create a local branch with it, but the performance impact is huge enough that it would seem prudent to get it into the main branch.

issue_title: TaskQueue behavior for duplicate tasks in a list during Queue.add()
issue_body:
I have some questions about the behavior of `taskqueue.Queue().add(task_list)` when 1 of the tasks is duplicate, but the others are not.

I am not aware of any documentation around the taskqueue API that explains this behavior - as such it seems undefined.

My concern is that adding a list of multiple tasks, where 1 may already have been tombstoned/created, may cause the other legitimate tasks to fail.

This affects the pipeline library in the following codeblock:

```python
try:
  taskqueue.Queue(self.queue_name).add(task_list)
except (taskqueue.TombstonedTaskError, taskqueue.TaskAlreadyExistsError):
  pass
```
> https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-pipelines/blob/bd9fa1e7000dc6af6fe704137cc53fa6d8cde427/python/src/pipeline/pipeline.py#L1659-L1663

Any clues? This may be a good question for @tkaitchuck.
This was brought up in issue #54.

issue_title: Can't use/pickle datastore Clients on google dataflow (apache beam)
issue_body:
I am attempting to use `datastore.Client()` from within a google cloud dataflow (apache beam) pipeline.

It attempts to pickle objects being passed around (lexically or arguments) to processing stages, but unfortunately the Client is not pickleable:
```
  File "lib/apache_beam/transforms/ptransform.py", line 474, in __init__
    self.args = pickler.loads(pickler.dumps(self.args))
  File "lib/apache_beam/internal/pickler.py", line 212, in loads

    return load(file)

I believe the correct fix is to discard the Connection when serializing, and rebuild it when deserialized.

This is a tricky cross-team interaction issue (only occurs for those pickling Clients, in my case: google-cloud-datastore *and* apache-beam google-dataflow), so not sure the proper place to file this. issue_title: Using a static json file for GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS is insecure
issue_body:
Forcing the client to reference a physical, static JSON file for secrets is insecure. It forces those secrets to be included in every bundle and on every app server. It temps developers to keep the secrets in version control. All around bad practice. 

Instead, this project should be able to use an individual environment variable for each key found in the JSON file. Those environment variables can be set securely, just for the process that is using them.

issue_title: Custom panic handler wipes out backtrace rendering
issue_body:
Inserting a panic in the native engine on master, you will see:
```
$ ./pants list 3rdparty/:
CRITICAL] panic at 'not yet implemented', src/rust/engine/src/scheduler.rs:40
CRITICAL] Please file a bug at https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/issues.
fatal runtime error: failed to initiate panic, error 5
Fatal Python error: Aborted
```

...due to the custom panic handler installed by https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/7319b21030f020ffefabebda30e5f6fbf48cf248/src/python/pants/engine/native.py#L721

But, annoyingly, this custom panic handler disables the highly useful `RUST_BACKTRACE` facility. Disabling the panic handler by commenting out its installation, I see:
```
thread '<unnamed>' panicked at 'not yet implemented', src/rust/engine/src/scheduler.rs:40
note: Run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` for a backtrace.
fatal runtime error: failed to initiate panic, error 5
Fatal Python error: Aborted
```
... and when I pass `RUST_BACKTRACE=1`, I get an actual useful backtrace pointing to the location of the panic.

issue_title: Redesign how JVM compile-time and runtime configuration is modeled
issue_body:
Currently they are semi-arbitrarily spread between subsystems such as `Java`, `JVM` and `ScalaPlatform`, and options on the `compile.zinc` task.  It would be nice to have a more rigorous and complete model that can distinguish between language settings, compiler settings and runtime settings, and that extends to other JVM languages (or maybe even languages in general) uniformly.

issue_title: fp16 training support
issue_body:
Purpose of this issue: This is a placeholder for a conversation regarding fp16 training support. I will be sharing my findings reagarding this issue here, and I suggest that if others have tried to make this happen, sharing the findings here would be much appreciated.

Background: fp16 training is important, particularly on Volta GPUs, which will soon be on the market. The fp16 FLOPS on Volta are almost 8x more compared to fp32 (~120 TFLOPS fp16 vs. ~15 TFLOPS fp32), and on Pascal that's still about 2x. 

Reproducibility: In the meantime, before Volta is widely available, the testing of whether the implementation supports fp16 training (irrespective of whether that affects training accuracy - just to check the "plumbing") can be done on Pascal GPUs, especially P100 (true fp16). It's possible to do it on Titan XP - Titan XP provides, emulated fp16, which is slower, but still allows development to verify MxNet and Sockeye support. From previous experiments using TensorFlow, we know that training with fp16 can generate the same accuracy as fp32 for various seq2seq models, including the public TensorFlow model.

issue_title: Cannot slice axis on tensor with MXNet backend
issue_body:
Minimum reproducible code:

```
import numpy as np

import keras
from keras import backend as K

# In Numpy
data = np.array([[[1,2,3], [4,5,6]]])
data.shape
(1,2,3)
data[:,-1,:].shape
(1,3)

# In Keras with MXNet backend
var1 = K.variable(data)
var1.shape
(1,2,3)
K.eval(var1[:,-1,:])
<<ERROR>>

Check failed: b < e (1 vs. 0) slicing with begin=[1]=1, end[1]=0, and step[1]=1 is invalid

```

Reason:

MXNet does not support slicing the axis with mx.sym.slice operator.
In KerasSymbol, __getitem__ method, we need to special case this kind of slicing and use mx.sym.slice_axis()

issue_title: template resource
issue_body:
One of the downsides of the micro services architecture model is that there is more overhead because the configuration, management and deployment tasks are duplicated for each service.  Our swagger schemas and Cloudformation templates are full of copy-and-pasted snippets.

In the example below, the commonality is extracted into `LambdaTemplate`, reducing the `PutFunction` to 3 lines and allowing the differences between the other functions to be more obvious.

```yaml
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31
Resources:
  LambdaTemplate:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::TemplateFunction
    Properties:
      Handler: index.handler
      Runtime: nodejs4.3
      Policies: AWSLambdaDynamoDBExecutionRole
      CodeUri: s3://bucketName/codepackage.zip
      Environment:
        Variables:
          TABLE_NAME: !Ref Table

  PutFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      # extra points if this can (optionally) be imported from an external file, even in a separate but shared repository
      Template: !Ref LambdaTemplate
  GetFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Template: !Ref LambdaTemplate
      Handler: index.getHandler
  DeleteFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Template: !Ref LambdaTemplate
      Events:
        Stream:
          Type: DynamoDB
          Properties:
            Stream: !GetAtt DynamoDBTable.StreamArn
            BatchSize: 100
            StartingPosition: TRIM_HORIZON

   DynamoDBTable:
     Type: AWS::DynamoDB::Table
     Properties: 
       AttributeDefinitions: 
         - AttributeName: id
           AttributeType: S
       KeySchema: 
         - AttributeName: id
           KeyType: HASH
       ProvisionedThroughput: 
         ReadCapacityUnits: 5
         WriteCapacityUnits: 5
       StreamSpecification:
         StreamViewType: streamview type
```

issue_title: cors: true
issue_body:
One thing that I like about serverless.com is that CORS can be enabled for an endpoint in one short line:

    cors: true

Currently, to enable CORS using `x-amazon-apigateway-integration`, it is necessary to:

- add a header to each response:

```yaml
responses:
        '200':
          description: Request successful
          headers:
            Access-Control-Allow-Origin:
              type: string
          schema:
            $ref: '#/definitions/Success'
        '400':
          description: Authentication failed
          headers:
            Access-Control-Allow-Origin:
              type: string
          schema:
            $ref: '#/definitions/Error'
```

- add responseParameters in `x-amazon-apigateway-integration`:

```yaml
default:
            statusCode: "200"
            responseParameters:
              method.response.header.Access-Control-Allow-Origin : "'*'"
          '.*"status":400.*':
            statusCode: '400'
            responseParameters:
              method.response.header.Access-Control-Allow-Origin : "'*'"
```

- add a massive `options` section:
```yaml
options:
      summary: CORS support
      description: |
        Enable CORS by returning correct headers
      responses:
        200:
          description: Default response for CORS method
          headers:
            Access-Control-Allow-Headers:
              type: string
            Access-Control-Allow-Methods:
              type: string
            Access-Control-Allow-Origin:
              type: string
      x-amazon-apigateway-integration:
        type: mock
        requestTemplates:
          application/json: |
            {"statusCode" : 200}
        responses:
          "default":
            statusCode: "200"
            responseParameters:
              method.response.header.Access-Control-Allow-Headers : "'Content-Type,X-Amz-Date,Authorization,X-Api-Key'"
              method.response.header.Access-Control-Allow-Methods : "'*'"
              method.response.header.Access-Control-Allow-Origin : "'*'"
            responseTemplates:
              application/json: |
                {}
```

...it'd be great if `Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31` could transform the swagger schema, automatically adding all of this if `cors: true` is present in the CloudFormation object

issue_title: Notorious form data issue
issue_body:
Currently, the way form data is stored is by combining all fields and values into a single string.
Each field/value combo is joined by a  ``:`` character and separated from the others via a newline.
This was an early not well thought out design decision because it means that any field that has a value with a ``:`` in it will cause Aldryn Forms to think that this value is it's own field.
This affects the importing and storing of form data as pointed out here https://github.com/aldryn/aldryn-forms/blob/master/aldryn_forms/models.py#L395.

I propose that instead of storing all field/values together, we either use a json field (with ordering) or an array field with the field/value pairs. This should be fairly simple to implement but my concern is backwards compatibility, this has to be backwards compatible which means we need a data migration that uses existing logic to separate each field/value pair from the stored string and then store them in the new json or array field, sadly there's no 100% reliable way we can actually tell if a field in the string is valid because of the issue mentioned above, to make it more reliable we could verify each found field with the form plugin fields but this would still not be 100% reliable because the idea behind form data was that the form plugin can be deleted without affecting anything in the stored data.

Thoughts?

issue_title: Add support for scoping services to AWS accounts
issue_body:
Let's say for instance we have multiple AWS accounts: sandbox, primary, security. We'll put confidant into the security account, and we'll store its KMS keys there. In the KMS key policy we'll allow security, development and production to use the authnz key, so that they can call get_service. The issue is at this point we're fully trusting the IAM policy of all accounts to properly limit access to services, whether or not those services are in their own account.

To support multi-account we'll need to add some information to the service, which allows us to scope the service to an account. A scoping that can work is: service -> account -> kms key. We'll create a KMS key for each account, then we'll add settings to KMS keys for each account. Our default will be backwards compatible, which is to allow the service to be fetched by any account (which will be our AUTH_KEY setting).

For now we'll only consider these settings for service auth. User auth will continue using the USER_AUTH_KEY and won't be scoped by account.

issue_title: Wrong default argument (--rm=False) for docker build
issue_body:
When running `docker build`, it (by default) removes intermediate containers if they succeeded, otherwise it leaves them behind. This is achieved by Docker internally setting `--rm=true` when building. You can verify this [here](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/build/).

The issue with `docker-py` is that it defaults to `--rm=false`, meaning it always leaves the intermediate containers, which, in addition to not being default behavior, is highly annoying to have to cleanup, especially when using `docker-py` with third party software, where there's no direct control over `docker-py`.

The fix is to simply set `rm=False` to `rm=True` here:
https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/master/docker/api/build.py#L17

issue_title: Executable permissions not synthesized when building from Windows
issue_body:
When building a Docker image the build context is tarred and sent to the Docker daemon. However when you build an image on a Windows client the build context's permissions are slightly messed up due to Unix and Windows differences. This is problematic because of missing executable permissions, which might render an image built from a Windows client unusable.

`docker build` just synthesizes the executable bit when you're building from Windows, however docker-py does not. This leads to very surprising situations where images built with `docker build` are perfectly fine, but images build with `docker-compose build` are broken because the entrypoint is not executable.

docker-py should follow `docker build`'s decision and just set the executable bit on every file in the tarball in order to prevent such breakage and to be consistent with the Docker CLI.

See the following for more details:

- "Unix file permissions and permission bits" in @ahmetalpbalkan's blog post about [Porting Docker Client to Windows](https://ahmetalpbalkan.com/blog/porting-docker-client-to-windows/)
- docker/docker#11047
- docker/docker@1a22418f9f1573ca8521831d52c8e0562cb3ef8f

issue_title: Updated story to reference the original story in JSON output
issue_body:
# What problem are we trying to solve?

Currently, there is no reference/link between an updated story that has been published and the original story. This is the case for the JSON output sent by Superdesk via the HTTP Push mechanism.

The problem with this is that client applications relying on the JSON output have no idea that an updated story is actually an update of a previously published story, they receive the story and see it just as any other new story.

Related user story: https://dev.sourcefabric.org/browse/SDESK-539

# How to test this

Pre-requisite: Make sure your Superdesk instance is configured to output a JSON version of the story on publishing.

1. Log into Superdesk, create a new story and publish it
2. Check out the JSON output of the published story. It should look something similar to [this](https://gist.github.com/hlmnrmr/a6d123e9df33e7973c9c3f6927ccfeb2)
3. Update the story and publish it
4. You will see now the JSON output sent to the client looks similar to [this](https://gist.github.com/hlmnrmr/8228e955b0c7e37cb96c72208fed8273).

# Solution

The solution is obviously to include the reference to the original story as part of the JSON output of the updated story, as you can see in [this Gist](https://gist.github.com/hlmnrmr/78d7a2164bc86cadd728dca2bbfb5d81)

With that, client applications can handle the reference in whichever way they prefer, for example:
- Overwriting the original story
- Publishing the update (and any subsequent updates) and next to it the original story

issue_title: as_future and generators
issue_body:
Both twisted and asyncio come with decorators which allow to write async code as if synchronous (`@inlineCallbacks` and `@coroutine` for reference). However it's easy to forget these decorators either while writing code or while refactoring code (e.g switching from a synchronous implementation of a func to async).

I argue that in 90% of all cases where a function returning a generator is passed into `as_future` the user actually just forgot the decorator. This can lead to nasty and hard to debug behaviour. E.g Autobahn's `ApplicationSession.onJoin` handler will simply not be invoked at all, but at the same time there isn't any feedback about this as well. The application will just continue as usual although code which was intended to be executed, was not. Due to the dynamic nature of Python, this can mean that actual errors might happen much much later. Cause and effect might not be that obvious to identify due to them being seperated by a very long period of time.

I propose that returning a generator from a function which is passed into `as_future` is to be considered an error. For the case that one actually wants to return a generator, the function should be decorated with `@txaio.returns_gen` which will wrap the generator in a `_Generator` marker object.

I'm aware of the fact that this is not consistent in behaviour with e.g `maybeDeferred` but I believe this the right place to implement that check. Otherwise it would mean that every piece of code which would need such a check would have to do it on its own which is just as easy to forget. Also this adds alot of boilerplate everytime.

issue_title: Make check for best model (and early stopping) use >, not >=
issue_body:
The way we're using `max` and `min` effectively does a `>=` check for whether current epoch is the best epoch, and we have that check in two different places (one for saving model weights, and one for early stopping).  We should use `>` instead of `>=`, and we should have only one check.

Benefits:
1. If two epochs get the same value for the validation metric, the earlier one is less likely to be overfit, and this change will make us keep the earlier model instead of the later model.
2. If you max out your validation metric, or just stop improving, you currently won't hit our early stopping criterion, because of the `>=`.  This will make you actually stop early.
3. Using one check instead of two simplifies the code.

issue_title: Tracking now fails in both 0.3.0 and 0.3.1 after recent Anaconda upload
issue_body:
Hi everyone,
I have a set of unit tests in my software for checking that tracking is performed correctly. These tests were working without problems with trackpy 0.3.0 for a couple of months until yesterday when the 0.3.1 build was pushed onto the Anaconda Cloud. Now they fail, seemingly because particles in different frames are not being merged onto the correct trajectories. The really crazy thing is that forcing Anaconda to use trackpy 0.3.0 *also* causes the tests to fail now. Am I making some kind of stupid mistake, or is tracking now broken in both versions?

# Expected behavior
An assertion error is not raised in the following code. This would mean that the two distinct particle trajectories were correctly identified.

```
import pandas as pd
import trackpy as tp

df = pd.DataFrame({'x' : [10, 20, 0, 100, 0, 110, 90, 90, 20, 110], 'y' : [10, 20, 20, 100, 0, 110, 110, 90, 0, 90], 'frame' : [0, 1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4, 5, 6, 6]})

mergedDF = tp.link_df(df, 25, memory = 2)

# There should be only two distinct values in the particles column: 0 and 1
assert mergedDF['particle'].max() == 1
```

# Actual behavior
The assertion fails because mergedDF['particle'].max() is 9. Every point is considered a distinct particle.

Thanks!

issue_title: DSC separate configuration file for environments etc
issue_body:
It has been proposed that we add a `--config` switch that points to a file to define various system environment needed to run DSC. The point here is to separate benchmark definition with execution environments. 

Currently there are two places to configure DSC: in section `DSC` and in `@CONF` for each module. Configurations specified in these places are mostly "portable" because they specify what software packages to use, where (in relative path) other script file and executables are located, etc. Additionally there is a configuration file for cluster that defines how DSC submits to a cluster system and the specifications for each module. See [here](https://github.com/stephenslab/dsc/blob/master/vignettes/one_sample_location/midway.yml) for an example.

Here I propose that we generalize cluster configuration and allow for module specific configuration, as well as global configurations. But we'll have to be careful with what we'd like to configure. I will list potential configurable items below and we should add to this list and evaluate before implement. Note that all these configures can be either global or module specific.

1. Work directory (default to current)
2. Global R interpreter and Python interpreter (default to `Rscript` and `python3` in system `PATH`)
3. Additional system PATH to append (default to empty)
4. Module specific script interpreter (default to either R, Python, or Shell depending on what type of module it is). Here we can also allow for `python2`, `julia`, even `matlab` but these interpreters will not provide "seamless" support as we've done for default R and Python. Rather, scripts will be executed as shell commands.

issue_title: v21 and v30 slb.virtual_server.vport.create does not respect tcp_template (and presumably udp_template)
issue_body:
Given an existing virtual_server on an A10 with V21 or V30 API support
when calling `slb.virtual_server.vport.create('foo', tcp_template=CONN-TIMEOUT-15S)`
or when calling `slb.virtual_server.vport.create('foo', udp_template=CONN-TIMEOUT-15S)`
then the tcp or udp templates should be applied like so...

```
!! slb virtual-server SG2NLHG558C1558-WEB-VS x.x.x.x /24
!!    port 80  tcp
!!       name foo
!!       template tcp CONN-TIMEOUT-15S
```
or
```
!! slb virtual-server SG2NLHG558C1558-WEB-VS x.x.x.x /24
!!    port 80  udp
!!       name foo
!!       template dup CONN-TIMEOUT-15S
```
This is so API users can control their own idle-timeout values on vports.

issue_title: Pin all installed packages to specific versions
issue_body:
In many package installations (such as [Ceph in store-base](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/c73c8f7e0fc96576f90a6cd4c2a66d08ba2dbae9/store/base/build.sh#L30)), we perform an `apt-get install` without pinning the installation to a specific release. We should be validating even minor changes in packages before bumping them. Without this change, there is also the possibility that some image builds will get newer builds and some will get older builds (depending on where they're built, the status of the apt mirror, etc.).

We need to do an assessment of all the package installations and ensure we're always specifying a specific version.

issue_title: Add `form_body` as an alias for `text_body` on post() and put() ?
issue_body:
Very often, especially when doing OAuth2 things, I'm working with the SDK and need to send a form body POST or PUT.
By virtue of the fact that `text_body=...` is passing through to `requests.Session.request(data=text_body,...)` I _know_ that I can put a dict into `text_body` to do form submission.

_This behavior is documented, but I still think it's too subtle._

How would we feel about having `form_body` which does one of the following?
1. Is a purely-cosmetic alias for `text_body`
2. Validates that it's getting a dict, then does the same thing as `text_body`
3. (2), plus have `text_body` log a warning if it does get a dict


I feel like minimally (1), and possibly (2) or (3) would make things "cleaner" without costing us much of anything.

issue_body:
### What is wrong?

The `validate_seal` method exists on the `Chain` class, however seal validation can and should be a `VM` level operation.

Move the `validate_seal` method to the `VM` class.  The `VM` class should call out to this within it's `validate_block` method.

issue_title: Allowing multiple peer subscribers can be problematic
issue_labels: [{'id': 889922027, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw4ODk5MjIwMjc=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ethereum/py-evm/labels/comp:%20p2p', 'name': 'comp: p2p', 'color': 'bfd4f2', 'default': False}, {'id': 983616278, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5ODM2MTYyNzg=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/ethereum/py-evm/labels/type:%20RFC%20/%20discussion%20/%20question', 'name': 'type: RFC / discussion / question', 'color': 'eeeeee', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

A `p2p.Peer` can have multiple subscribers, and each of them will get a copy of every msg sent by the remote (which allows us to have separate services handling different subsets of messages), but that can be problematic if both subscribers request/process the same type of data as the data requested by one subscriber will be delivered to the other as well, and might interfere other's operation.

As an example, we currently have `LightChainSyncer` and `LightPeerChain` as peer subscribers when we run a light node, and they can both request/process `BlockHeader`s, and if the latter requests some headers while the former is in the middle of a sync with a peer, it may try to validate/process those headers and if that fails (which can happen when the headers are further ahead of our local chain head), it will cause the sync with that peer to be aborted. Although this is not that big a deal given that another sync will be triggered as soon as one of our peers notify us of a new block, it is something we need to be aware of. And we must consider other cases where similar issues might happen

The above can be seen in practice when running `tests/p2p/test_lightchain_integration.py` as it requests the remote's head (via `LightPeerChain`) while `LightChainSyncer` is in the middle of a sync, which causes the sync to be aborted.

### How can it be fixed

In the case above, it could be fixed by having `LightChainSyncer` keep the `request_id`s used in the `GetBlockHeader` requests sent by itself and only processing the replies for those, but that won't work for services using the `ETH` protocols because there the requests don't include a `request_id`

I need to think more about this, just writing it down now so I don't forget about it

issue_title: ssl_verify: false seems to be ignored
issue_body:
The `ssl_verify` option in conda doesn't seem to work in conda 4.4.8+. (I haven't tested preceding versions, just 4.4.8 and 4.4.10, but it certainly used to work some time ago.)

I have the option enabled currently:

```none
> conda config --get ssl_verify

--set ssl_verify False
```

And yet:

```none
> conda update foo

An HTTP error occurred when trying to retrieve this URL.
HTTP errors are often intermittent, and a simple retry will get you on your way.
SSLError(MaxRetryError('HTTPSConnectionPool(host=\'an-internal-server\', port=443):
Max retries exceeded with url: /repository/some-repo/win-64/repodata.json
(Caused by SSLError(SSLError("bad handshake: Error([(\'SSL routines\', \'ssl3_get_server_certificate\', \'certificate verify failed\')],)",),))',),)
```

If I access this same URL using `requests` directly instead then it's fine:

```python
url = "https://an-internal-server/repository/some-repo/win-64/repodata.json"
requests.get(url, verify=False)
<Response [200]>
```

This may be Windows-specific, as it seems fine on my Mac with identical configuration. Here's the user agent string reported by `conda info`:

```
user-agent : conda/4.4.10 requests/2.18.4 CPython/3.6.4 Windows/7 Windows/6.1.7601
```

issue_title: .sh extension required for environment variable scripts on *nix
issue_body:
The [docs on how to customize environment variables](https://conda.io/docs/using/envs.html#saved-environment-variables) state that "Scripts can be given any name", but through trial and error I found that at least on OSX, they *must* have a `.sh` extension, else they don't get executed.

Either the docs should specify this (for all OSes) or, even better, the restriction on a specific extension should be lifted (and then the docs would be correct).

issue_title: ENH: add --force option to conda env create
issue_body:
Both conda env create and conda create need a --force option that will overwrite the environment if it already exists.

conda could warn the user and prompt for confirmation but must also allow for --yes option to not prompt so conda can be used effectively in automation scripts that won't have human interaction.

issue_title: conda install <package> incorrectly says package is installed
issue_body:
I have a package that I maintain in a custom channel (own server).

When I download conda anew I can

```
<install conda>
conda install libnnpdf
```

perfectly fine. But if I try to remove it and install it again, it says it is installed even thought it is not. Same thing happens with a dependency of that package. I have purged all reference to that package from the anaconda directory that I can find, and so I don't understand  how it decides it's installed:

```
conda clean --all
rm pkgs/urls.txt
rm conda-meta/history
...

issue_title: Code folding issues with inline PERFORM
issue_body:
The following issues can be seen in the attached image:
![marked_text](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6699539/14559536/cb1a7f38-0309-11e6-8388-f24066e0e610.PNG)

* inline ``PERFORM`` cannot be collapsed
* ``END-PERFORM`` is sometimes used as end marker for ``IF``

It's likely not that easy to recognize a ``PERFORM`` as inline as the simplest rule is "if PERFORM is followed by  a procedure name it is an out-of-line PERFORM" and OCIDE doesn't know anything about procedure names. But as OCIDE knows about "tags" (procedure names, variable names) it *may* be possible to recognize ``PERFORM tag`` as inline perform.

issue_title: Should 'fields' be a list or a string?
issue_body:
The client args for the `fields` param says `A comma-separated list of fields to return as part of a hit`. That's not super clear, but suggests to me that it expects a string (since specifying the delimiter implies a pseudo-list encoded as a string. However, this argument seems passed-through to Elasticsearch and appears to work if I send a list here (`'fields': ['first_name', 'last_name']`), but not if I send a string (`'fields': "first_name,last_name"`).

Either way I think we'll have a change to make in haystack to support this feature because it's currently assuming the client wants a string containing a space-delimited list of fields [1]. I figured I'd confirm the expectations here before issuing a pull request over there. Thanks!

[1] https://github.com/toastdriven/django-haystack/blob/master/haystack/backends/elasticsearch_backend.py#L275

issue_title: Specifying 'disabled' for '--endpoint-status' creates an endpoint in the 'Enabled' state
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
The '--endpoint-status' argument value of 'az network traffic-manager endpoint create' is case sensitive and does not behave the way one would expect. If 'Disabled' is specified as the value, the new endpoint has 'Disabled' as the status. If 'disabled' is specified, the new endpoint has 'Enabled' as the status.

**To Reproduce**
Create a new traffic manager endpoint using 'az network traffic-manager endpoint create' and specify '--endpoint-status disabled' as one of the arguments. Observe that the newly created endpoint is in the 'Enabled' state even though 'disabled' was requested.

**Expected behavior**
Specifying an invalid value for the endpoint status such as 'invalid' will cause the Azure CLI to print the message that the only valid values are 'Enabled' and 'Disabled'. But the CLI will accept 'disabled' as a value but then treat it as if it was actually 'Enabled'. Either the CLI should reject 'disabled' as invalid or it should accept it and treat it as 'Disabled'.

**Environment summary**
Azure CLI 2.0.33 on both Windows and Linux

issue_title: PUBLIC NOTICE: Azure Storage Python Revamp
issue_body:
The Azure Storage team is taking ownership of the storage runtime library which is present in this repository and released as the azure-storage package on pypi. As promised in the [package change announcement](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/440), there will be many new storage features coming soon.

The first round of changes will be the largest and will bring the library up to the newest service version. As it currently stands, the library supports very few features which were released after 2012. To best support all the new features released on the service in the past 3 years, we will be making breaking changes to bring the Python storage library up to date. Some of these changes include append blob support, file service support, table batch refactors, replacing AtomPub with JSON in table, account SAS, improved response parsing and many other small features and API updates. 

All of this is being done in the dev branch and we will be maintaining ChangeLog and BreakingChanges files so you can see a summary of what’s happening. Note that this branch should not be considered stable!

After bringing the library up to the latest version we will do a preview release. Then we will begin adding additional improvements. These may include items such as built-in MD5 calculation, automatically following continuation tokens on list APIs, improved queue message encoding support, and many others, in addition to improvements the community suggests. 

We’d love to hear from you!

Thanks,
The Azure Storage Team

issue_title: check-docstring-first can't handle unicode
issue_body:
We have some application where there are Unicode characters (German umlauts, e.g. "ü") in some strings. Strangely I could not reproduce the issue in my dev environment, only in my testing environment.

I tested in a Docker container `buildpack-deps:xenial`.

```
# on the host
docker run -it buildpack-deps:xenial /bin/bash
# in the container
apt update && apt install python3-pip
# here I'd install pre-commit with other things via pip3
pre-commit install
pre-commit autoupdate
pre-commit run --all-files

Check docstring is first.................................................Failed
hookid: check-docstring-first

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/root/.pre-commit/repomdnrbb1l/py_env-python3/bin/check-docstring-first", line 11, in <module>
    load_entry_point('pre-commit-hooks==0.9.1', 'console_scripts', 'check-docstring-first')()
  File "/root/.pre-commit/repomdnrbb1l/py_env-python3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pre_commit_hooks/check_docstring_first.py", line 60, in main
    contents = io.open(filename).read()
  File "/root/.pre-commit/repomdnrbb1l/py_env-python3/lib/python3.5/encodings/ascii.py", line 26, in decode
```

A (not so) quick [search for unicode characters](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/3001177/how-do-i-grep-for-all-non-ascii-characters-in-unix) resulted in:
```plain
ag -r "[\x80-\xFF]" .

260:        return "Nicht Gen��gend"
309:            return (4, "Gen��gend")
```

I am wondering if this is because `python --version` shows the 2.7 Python (`2.7.12`).

issue_title: Hooks pre-fetching
issue_body:
Hi,

I want to suggest introducing a command for downloading all required hooks forcefully. The use case is simple:
1. I pack everything needed for testing into container
2. I deploy that into CI
3. It gets built and tests are ran

I'd like to separate fetching of the hooks and pre-installing them into a build step. This would allow me use caching for containers, saving time for tests.

P.S. Perhaps it's needed to add some argument to `run` command, so that it won't try downloading stuff when executing tests.

issue_title: Limit requests per timeframe to each domain
issue_body:
## Long story short

Currently I can use limit_per_host to limit the number of simultaneous connections to a host. How can I limit the number of requests per timeframe (eg. minute) to each host?

## Expected behaviour

A parameter probably on TCPConnector that sets a limit to the number of requests that can be made to a host in a particular timeframe. I could imagien doing what was done for timeouts and introducing a new class to put it all together eg. ConnectorLimit:

    limit = aiohttp.ConnectorLimit(total=100, limit_per_host=1, limit_per_time_per_host=5, time=60)
    conn = aiohttp.TCPConnector(limit=limit, loop=loop)

In the above:
- total is the parameter limit from TCPConnector
- limit_per_host is the parameter limit_per_host from TCPConnector
- limit_per_time_per_host and time are new parameters
- time is in seconds
- so with limit_per_time_per_host=5, it means maximum 5 connections can be made to a host in 60 seconds

## Actual behaviour

It's not possible right now.

## Steps to reproduce

    conn = aiohttp.TCPConnector(limit=100, limit_per_host=1, loop=loop)
    timeout = aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=5*60)
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession(connector=conn, timeout=timeout, loop=loop) as session:
    ....

## Your environment

aiohttp 3.3.2 client

issue_title: Improve middleware documentation
issue_body:
Current docs are not exhausting clean (#2848 for example).

https://docs.aiohttp.org/en/stable/web_advanced.html#middlewares section should have an example for *middleware factory* (a function that creates a middleware with passed arguments).

*middleware factory* conflicts with a factory for old-style middlewares, I think dropping *old-styles* from docs is ok (but we should support this old style for a while, #2851)

Document that *normalize path middleware* https://docs.aiohttp.org/en/stable/web_reference.html#normalize-path-middleware is actually a factory.

issue_title: Subsecond timeouts
issue_body:
## Long story short

It seems that aiohttp rounds up epoch times and timeouts using `math.ceil`, effecitvely preventing timeouts of less than 1 second.

## Expected behaviour

Subsecond timeouts should be supported, at least at the millisecond level.

## Actual behaviour

Subsecond timeouts are not respected.

## Steps to reproduce

I stumbled on this issue while trying unsuccessfully to write a unit test that tests timeouts, until I came across some related tests in aiohttp itself that [patch `aiohttp.helpers.ceil` to be a no-op](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/tests/test_client_functional.py#L580). Without the patch these tests (usually) fail.

## Your environment

`aiohttp==2.0.7`

issue_body:
## Long story short
 
I migrated from aiohttp 1.0 to aiohttp 2.0. It seems to work correctly but it looks unstable.

## Expected behaviour

No critical error.

## Actual behaviour

I got a critical error in data received : 

```
ERROR:root:Exception in callback _QEventLoop.__notifier_cb_wrapper({7: <PyQt5.QtCore...x7f27e9543d38>, 26: <PyQt5.QtCore...x7f27d1ca5c18>, 27: <PyQt5.QtCore...x7f27d1c275e8>, 30: <PyQt5.QtCore...x7f27d1a9c048>, ...}, <PyQt5.QtCore...x7f27d1aa2dc8>, 106, <bound method..., bufsize=0>>>, ())

    self._tail += data
TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +=: 'NoneType' and 'bytes'
```
## Steps to reproduce
I'm not sure what is the cause of this bug, but by analyzing the code I found some weird incoherencies. Here I found the lines 
```python
        if self._tail:
            data, self._tail = self._tail, None
self.data_received(data)

Where ` self._tail` is set to `None` before calling `self.data_received`.

issue_title: Read in submission timestamps from `timestamp.txt`
issue_body: `nbgrader submit` saves the timestamp into a file called `timestamp.txt`. `nbgrader autograde` should automatically look for this file, and if it finds it, parse the date and save it into the database.

issue_title: Switch from preact to react
issue_body:
Now that React is MIT licensed, we can switch over to using React proper in the classic notebook

One of the reasons is that React has an [actual UMD bundle that we can get via bower](https://github.com/reactjs/react-bower), making React get loaded explicitly with requirejs instead of the current preact global.

Another important reason for us to switch to React instead of `preact` is the existence of the `blocklist` and `safelist` for particular elements. `vdom.h('script', 'alert("hey")')` does not run with the react based version of the vdom mimetype whereas the preact one will. We certainly want the XSS protections in place that React makes available.

issue_title: Error fails silently for `asyncio tasks`
issue_body:
Using the notebook, an exception is expected for the following code:

```python
import asyncio
from datetime import datetime

# coroutine function
async def anything(i):
    print(i, datetime.now())
    await asyncio.sleep(i)
    err                                                    # exceptions seem to be ignored

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
tasks = [loop.create_task(anything(i)) for i in range(1, 7)]
loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.wait(tasks)) 
loop.close()
print('F', datetime.now())
```
However, no exception is raised after the cell is executed. As far as I have tested, expected errors in coroutines are silenced for several implementations of asyncio tasks.

I have confirmed in console ipython on two machines that the latter code also runs without errors, **but actually generates the following traceback only *after quitting* ipython.**

```bash
In [2]: 
Do you really want to exit ([y]/n)? 
Task exception was never retrieved
future: <Task finished coro=<anything() done, defined at <ipython-input-1-fd581dbf29e1>:5> exception=NameError("name 'err' is not defined",)>
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/asyncio/tasks.py", line 239, in _step
    result = coro.send(None)
  File "<ipython-input-1-fd581dbf29e1>", line 8, in anything
    err                                                    # exceptions seem to be ignored
NameError: name 'err' is not defined
```

This implicit exception behavior is strange, and  I believe is possibly a BUG.  Exceptions should be raised in explicitly in the notebook and console. 

Windows 7 | 64-bit | Python 3.5.1 | IPython 4.1.2 | notebook 4.2.0

issue_title: GPU Support through CuPy
issue_body:
Hi,

I think you have an awesome library here, but the lack of GPU support makes it a tough sell. It may be fairly straightforward to add GPU support to autograd using the CuPy library from Chainer (http://chainer.readthedocs.org/en/stable/index.html). CuPy is a numpy clone that runs on the GPU. In Chainer, you can write device-agnostic code by importing and using an 'xp' module, which can refer to either numpy of cupy. Since cupy has the same API as numpy, the code using xp does not have to know about GPUs and CPUs.

Perhaps you can do a similar thing for autograd: replace all 'np' in the autograd code by 'xp' and let the user decide which library to use. Do you see any problems with this approach? The cupy code is MIT licensed, so there should not be an issue there.

Best,
Taco

issue_title: hessian + flatten_func: singleton dimension
issue_body:
Hi,

I find that when 'hessian' is used with 'flatten_func', it will add an extra dimension.

For example,

`
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd.util import flatten_func
from autograd import hessian

def objective(param):
    return np.sum(np.square(param))

init_param = np.random.randn(2) # param is a 2-dim vector
hessian(objective)(init_param)
`

will produce a 2x2 matrix

`
array([[ 2.,  0.],
       [ 0.,  2.]])
`

While,

`
init_param = np.random.randn(2,2) # param is a 2 x 2 matrix
flattened_objective, unflatten, flattened_init_param = flatten_func(objective, init_param)
hessian(flattened_objective)(flattened_init_param)
`

will produce a 1x4x4 tensor (should it be a 4x4 matrix instead?)

`
array([[[ 2.,  0.,  0.,  0.],
        [ 0.,  2.,  0.,  0.],
        [ 0.,  0.,  2.,  0.],
        [ 0.,  0.,  0.,  2.]]])
`

It's easy to fix by 'squeeze', but is there a reason for it (it's not a bug but a feature somehow?)

issue_title: Brackets enclosing values (i. e. {{Institutional Author}}) are removed
issue_body:
The parser does a little bit too much cleanup. Entire values enclosed in brackets are stripped of the enclosing brackets. There are numerous instances where bracketing entire values are appropriate, for example with institutional authors. An example:

```
@book{SillyWalks1970,
 author = {{Ministry of Silly Walks}},
 location = {London},
 publisher = {We Publish Anything},
 title = {Handbook of Silly Walks},
 year = {1970}
}
```

The above bibtex entry yields (linebreaks added for readability):

```
[{u'publisher': u'We Publish Anything',
 u'title': u'Handbook of Silly Walks',
 u'author': u'Ministry of Silly Walks',
 'ID': 'SillyWalks1970',
 u'location': u'London',
 u'year': u'1970',
 'ENTRYTYPE': u'book'}]
```

Value of `author` should be `u'{Ministry of Silly Walks}'`, with brackets left untouched.

issue_title: From 7a04e74 to b07986f Attack_minimap not working
issue_body:
From Commit 7a04e74 (currently latest master branch commit as of 2018.05.06) to Commit b07986f (currently latest dev branch commit as of 2018.05.06), `actions.FUNCTIONS.Attack_minimap` doesn't work.  When called in my created agent as:
```python
return actions.FUNCTIONS.Attack_minimap('now', self.transformLocation(int(x) + (x_offset * 8), int(y) + (y_offset * 8)))
```
Once the agent hits the return, there is a long output of trace-backs that end at `lib.features.func_call_ability` which gives `ValueError: Unknown ability_id: 3674, type: cmd_quick. Likely a bug.`  Pastebin link with full bash CLI trace-back: [https://pastebin.com/FAVaQX7t](https://pastebin.com/FAVaQX7t). 

The issue seems to be with how the command is stored in the `actions.py` file, where stored in the `FUNCTIONS` variable, `FUNCTIONS` stores `Function.ability` objects in the notation `Function.ability(func_id, name, func, ab_id, general_id)`.  

However, `Attack_minimap` only has 4 items, not 5 like most of the rest of the items generated by `gen_actions.py`.  I have not tested this issue with any of the other commands that only contain 4 items, although since this failed in `lib.features.func_call_ability`, it is possible this issue is only relevant to abilities that both have only 4 properties and are `cmd_minimap` type commands, as I suspect, or it may cover a larger scope.  From `func_id` 12-20, they share `general_id` 3674.  Since `Attack_minimap` is missing `ab_id`, `general_id` somehow gets read in as `ab_id` in `lib.features.func_call_ability` and everything crashes, even though `lib.features.func_call_ability` does not throw this issue in the current master branch, with `FUNCTIONS` appearing unchanged from 7a04e74 to b07986f.  In the `FUNCTIONS` variable, changing `general_id` of `Attack_minimap` from 3674 to something like 13 or 14 (numbers specifically tested) causes the program not to crash any-more, but  `Attack_minimap` will not execute in any way, as the units will be selected, no action taken, then the step continued.  


Other relevant information: 
OS: Ubuntu 16.04
Starcraft II Patch Level: 3.16
map: Simple64
agent_race: terran
use_feature_units: True, *Edit: also fails with False*

issue_title: Give generated entities a server config object
issue_body:
When `create_missing` is called on an entity, it fills in all fields that are
required and that do not have values. When filling in a field such as an
`IntegerField` or a `StringField`, the process of creating a bogus value is
straight-forward. When filling in a `OneToOneField` or `OneToManyField`, the
process is more delicate. The correct entity class must be instantiated with an
appropriate `nailgun.config.ServerConfig` object, and all of the dependent
entity's required fields must also be filled in. Currently, method
`nailgun.entity_mixins.EntityCreateMixin.create_missing` does not do that.
Currently, the method instantiates a new entity without passing in a
`ServerConfig` object (causing the new object to search for a server config) and
`create_missing` is not called on the new entity.

issue_title: Drop support for Python 2
issue_body:
Oscar should drop support for Python 2 in version 1.7. Version 1.6 (which we hope to release soon) will continue to support Python 2 for as long as Django 1.11 (which supports Python 2) is maintained, but will only contain security and bug fixes. 

New features added to version 1.7 will  support Python 3 only.

This will enable us to simplify and rationalise the existing code base. It will also ensure that we are not blocked from upgrading upstream libraries, several of which have also switched to supporting Python 3 only.

issue_title: $(PROJECTDIR) Set Incorrectly in .NET Core Projects
issue_body:
I began investigating problems with EF migrations which led me down a diagnostic path that eventually led to me discovering that PROJECTDIR is not set correctly on any .net core projects. Entity Framework depends on PROJECTDIR to resolve its depsfile, so this seems to be breaking some things. I'm not sure if this is specific to my machine, but I have reproduced it across different .NET core projects.

# General

I am using Visual Studio 2017 15.7.3 along with .NET Core 2.1.0/SDK version 2.1.3 on Windows 10 Pro build 17134.81 / Version 1803.
The steps to reproduce this are as follows. I have a main directory for my solutions, C:\Git\. I create a test solution, Test. Inside the solution, I create four projects: .NET Core 2.1 Console App (test1), ASP.NET Core Web Application 2.1 (test2), .NET Framework 4.5 Console App (test3), and ASP.NET Web Application (.NET Framework) (test4). Do nothing to the projects except hit build, then go to the properties for each project in the solution, then to build events, then edit either pre- or post-build button, then click the macros button to list the variables for the project. Check the ProjectDir variable. 

For the .NET Core projects, I see a ProjectDir of "C:\Git\" whereas for the non-Core projects, I see C:\Git\Test\test3\ and C:\Git\Test\test4\, respectively.

This probably wouldn't matter to me, but msbuild is used to elicit ProjectDir from the project file here: https://github.com/aspnet/EntityFrameworkCore/blob/908491ca0f55a27c43a7b5b91a92a3714f7c89cd/src/dotnet-ef/Project.cs#L131. That code, in turn, is used here https://github.com/aspnet/EntityFrameworkCore/blob/9f0a44e29ee28fee57739c2f4bccaa4e46e74b4f/src/dotnet-ef/RootCommand.cs#L98 to generate the depsfile location. Thus, any EF commands executed on a .NET Core project find themselves using the wrong ProjectDir, and end up executing dotnet exec --depsfile C:\Git\bin\Debug\netcoreapp2.1\test1.deps.json, which should really be dotnet exec --depsfile C:\Git\Test\test1\bin\Debug\netcoreapp2.1\test1.deps.json.

issue_title: Extremely simple http-01 in apache
issue_body:
How about implementing `http-01` for Apache? After writing #1268 I realized that the authenticator would not even have to touch users config - just drop a file in `/etc/apache2/conf-enabled/` with the following contents:

```
<IfModule mod_headers.c>
    <LocationMatch "/.well-known/acme-challenge/*">
        Header set Content-Type "application/jose+json"
        DocumentRoot /var/lib/letsencrypt/apache-http01-root
    </LocationMatch>
</IfModule>
```

reload the daemon, drop necessary resources in `/var/lib/letsencrypt/apache-http01-root`, and voila!

The biggest advantage is that we might even get away without read access (parsing) of the existing configs, not even mentioning write access :) That means less scope for errors (a lot of users don't even get to the authorization stage, because of MisconfigurationErrors raised in the parsing step).

Possible problems:
- users don't have `/etc/apache/conf-enabled` by default
- `DocumentRoot` seems to be allowed there (https://httpd.apache.org/docs/2.2/mod/core.html#documentroot), but if it's not, then we can drop the stanza and try to read the config file and find existing `public_html`

@jdkasten?

issue_title: Consumer unable to read current offset position until one message is consumed for each partition assigned
issue_body:
Description
===========

Given a consumer group that has consumed all available messages. When a new consumer starts up and subscribes to a topic if there are no new messages in the topic there is no way for that consumer to establish its current position. 

the assignment callback provides the list of partitions but all offsets are `-1001`. `consumer.position(consumer.assignment())` also return `-1001`.

This issue was brought up in #118 and caused the exact issue described. A topic with no new message and at least one worker restart will cause committed offsets being ttl'd and the worker reprocessing then entire topic.

Becuase there is no ConsumerGroup api support in the admin api requested in #223 there is no way to hack around this.

issue_title: Coveralls doesn't link files correctly [minor]
issue_body:
The reason is that our `.travis.yml` recipe is designed to test the *installed* package rather than just the local checkout. On the one hand, this is nice, because detects problems due to modules not being included in the installation. On the other hand, it obviously confuses coveralls which cannot match the absolute path name of the installed file in the virtualenv to the file in the github repository.

Solutions:
- ignore?
- change directory before executing tests?
- just test the local checkout?

issue_title: Async + multiple clients
issue_body:
Just a thought. The RPC service could be made asynchronous, so that requests don't block.

If this were ever implemented: it would probably be easy to allow multiple clients to connect to the same libmadx service process and let each receive events.

This allows to share a MAD-X interpretor among multiple client processes.

issue_title: Visual Recognition API - calling classify twice with the same file object fails
issue_body:
If you open an image in Python as a file object and pass it to `VisualRecognitionV3.classify` multiple times, it fails every time after the first call.

Here is an example:
```
from watson_developer_cloud import VisualRecognitionV3

api_key="your API key"
custom_classifier_id="your custom classifier ID"
latest_version = VisualRecognitionV3.latest_version
vr = VisualRecognitionV3(latest_version, api_key=api_key)

#######################################

image_file = open("image.jpg", "rb")
for i in range(1, 4): # i = 1, 2, 3
	try: vr.classify(images_file=image_file)
	except: print "general classification attempt %d failed" % i

#######################################

image_file = open("image.jpg", "rb")
for i in range(4, 7): # i = 4, 5, 6
	try: vr.classify(images_file=image_file)
	except: print "general classification attempt %d failed" % i

#######################################

image_file = open("image.jpg", "rb")
for i in range(1, 4): # i = 1, 2, 3
	try: vr.classify(images_file=image_file, classifier_ids=[custom_classifier_id])
	except: print "custom classification attempt %d failed" % i

#######################################

image_file = open("image.jpg", "rb")
for i in range(4, 7): # i = 4, 5, 6
	try: vr.classify(images_file=image_file, classifier_ids=[custom_classifier_id])
	except: print "custom classification attempt %d failed" % i
```

This prints the following:
```
general classification attempt 2 failed
general classification attempt 3 failed
general classification attempt 5 failed
general classification attempt 6 failed
custom classification attempt 2 failed
custom classification attempt 3 failed
custom classification attempt 5 failed
custom classification attempt 6 failed
```

Shouldn't it be possible to pass the same file in?

Thanks!

issue_title: Add overrides from environment variables
issue_body:
Currently `powerline-config tmux setenv` is responsible for colors and dividers, but it lacks support for `--theme_option` or `--config`. I think it is needed to use some variable *other* then `POWERLINE_COMMAND` to specify configuration overrides both to `powerline-config` and `powerline`. Possibilities:

1. Share code in `powerline/commands/*.py` and call both `POWERLINE_CONFIG_COMMAND` and `POWERLINE_COMMAND` like `$POWERLINE_…COMMAND $POWERLINE_COMMAND_ARGS`.
2. Use environment variables like those already used in zsh/zpython to specify configuration.
3. Use `$POWERLINE_COMMAND --setup` to set up tmux, not `$POWERLINE_CONFIG_COMMAND tmux setenv`.

I personally do not like the third variant and other variants are inconsistent with the current practices. Thus the suggestion:

- [x] Make `POWERLINE_COMMAND` and `POWERLINE_CONFIG_COMMAND` always point to the executable only (incompatible with tests and documentation), make sure that shells treat them as one argument. Ref #1124.
- [x] Add and use environment variable `POWERLINE_CONFIG_OVERRIDES`. If possible, make it colon-separated (with the respect for the nesting) array like this:

        export POWERLINE_CONFIG_OVERRIDES='common.term_truecolor=true:common.additional_escapes=tmux:ext.tmux={"top_theme":"unicode"}'

   (note: colon between `top_theme` and `unicode` does not separate the values).

   Some solution for escaping colons may be accepted for performance reasons. It may also be practical to just ignore the problem since colons are inevitable only in dictionaries and dictionary entries with dictionaries themselves can already be defined (but not inside the lists in case overrides are used to override segments list).

    Another possibility: simply use not colon-separated list, but either semicolon-separated one or double-colon-separated. JSON does not use semicolon for anything and it may be escaped inside strings using `\x3b`.
- [x] Add and use environment variable `POWERLINE_THEME_OVERRIDES` with same parsing rules as above. Ref #1126.
- [x] Make zsh/zpython support the above two points, but leave it possible to have associative array instead.
- [x] Add and use `POWERLINE_CONFIG_PATHS` environment variable which is a colon-separated list. In zsh/zpython it may be a list like it currently is.
- [x] Define and follow what does empty entry in the above colon-separated variables mean. It should mean “no entry”, not “current directory (applicable for `POWERLINE_CONFIG_PATHS`)” or something like “drop previous configuration (applicable for `POWERLINE_…_OVERRIDES`)”.
- [x] Make `powerline-config` accept these overrides.
- [x] Add tests that make sure that in ipython and Vim these overrides are ignored. They have better facilities for defining overrides.
- [x] Fix tests.
- [x] Fix documentation.

issue_title: --excludeDirRegex does not work when source is B2
issue_body:
The new filtering that lets you exclude an entire directory works in the `LocalFolder` class, but not the `B2Folder` class.

I think there are two possible approaches to fixing it: (1) change B2Folder to simulate the existence of directories, and check them for exclusion, or (2) extend `ScanPoliciesManager.should_exclude_file` to also test whether any of the directories in the path are excluded.  I like #2, but I think it would need optimization to avoid checking every parent directory of every file.

issue_title: b2 sync does not preserve modification dates in docker environment
issue_body: It seems downloading from b2 (b2 sync to local) creates files with current timestamp on a Linux Ubuntu machine (docker image ubuntu 16.04). In turn, specifying `--replaceNewer` to keep the remote and local copies in sync as suggested by the b2 cli, keeps files re-syncing again and again. `--skipNewer` is a rough workaround because it leaves local modifications in place, `--compareVersions sha1` is not yet added afaik and `--compareVersion size` is still a bit off.

issue_title: Getting field list in a `SchemaMeta` extension
issue_body:
The title might be a sort of XY-problem, so I'll explain the X-part first.
I want to generate data classes automagically from the Schema class, so that the following would work without writing redundant code:

    class UserSchema(MySmartSchemaExtension):
        firstName = fields.Str(attribute='first_name')
        lastName = fields.Str(attribute='last_name')

    user = UserSchema.DataType('John', 'Doe')
    assert user.first_name == 'John'
    assert user.last_name == 'Doe'

That seems trivial by extending `SchemaMeta` and generating a `namedtuple` or anything similar within `__new__`. However, for that purpose I'd need a full list of declared fields, which is available only as a protected field. Is there any workaround, or am I out of the right track completely? Would it be even better to generate such a type per schema instance (`UserSchema().DataType`) rather than per class?

issue_title: miniterm.py does not use exclusive access for native serial ports
issue_body:
(The below applies to Linux / POSIX; haven't checked how it behaves on Windows)

Running `miniterm.py` while another process already has the same native serial port open will cause both processes to receive only garbage (or more accurately each will only receive parts of the data), even if the other process uses `flock()` for exclusive access. Since pyserial 3.3 the API offers the `exclusive` option to avoid several processes having the same native port open at the same time. However `miniterm.py` doesn't use this API, neither by default nor via an option.

issue_title: klucher results different between pandas and numpy
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
call `pvlib.irradiance.klucher` with `dhi`, `ghi`, `solar_zenith`, `solar_azimuth` as `pd.Series` and you get different answers than if you user `np.array`

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import pvlib

surface_tilt = 32.0
surface_azimuth = 180.0
```
#### NumPy Array
```python
nda_apparent_zenith = np.array([
    124.039086298488, 82.8545704390216,10.5641356171064, 72.4168712240143
])  # solar zenith
nda_azimuth = np.array([
    352.695506991832, 66.7141033780672, 144.76567753998, 287.041041278537
])  # solar azimuth
nda_ghi = np.array([0, 79.8826605157945, 1042.48125969315, 257.25820554723])
nda_dhi = np.array([0, 40.4911557248134, 118.458383320548, 62.0343739267192])
# calculate klucher transposition
sky_np = pvlib.irradiance.klucher(
    surface_tilt=surface_tilt,
    surface_azimuth=surface_azimuth,
    dhi=nda_dhi,
    ghi=nda_ghi,
    solar_zenith=nda_apparent_zenith,
    solar_azimuth=nda_azimuth
)
# array([  0.        ,  37.41480162, 109.45839488,  57.32125332])
```
#### Pandas Series
```python
times = pd.date_range(start='2014-06-24', freq='6H', periods=4, tz='US/Arizona')
apparent_zenith = pd.Series(nda_apparent_zenith, index=times)
azimuth = pd.Series(nda_azimuth, index=times)
ghi = pd.Series(nda_ghi, index=times)
dhi = pd.Series(nda_dhi, index=times)
# calculate klucher transposition
sky_pd = pvlib.irradiance.klucher(
    surface_tilt=surface_tilt,
    surface_azimuth=surface_azimuth,
    dhi=dhi,
    ghi=ghi,
    solar_zenith=apparent_zenith,
    solar_azimuth=azimuth
)
# 2014-06-24 00:00:00-07:00      0.000000
# 2014-06-24 06:00:00-07:00     38.286085
# 2014-06-24 12:00:00-07:00    112.287559
# 2014-06-24 18:00:00-07:00     59.009638
# Freq: 6H, dtype: float64
```

**Expected behavior**
There is no difference in results between using Pandas and NumPy

**Versions:**
 - ``pvlib.__version__``: 0.5.1, 0.5.2
 - ``pandas.__version__``: 0.23.3
- `np.__version__`: 1.14.5
 - Python: 3.6.6 |Anaconda, Inc.| (default, Jun 28 2018, 11:27:44) [MSC v.1900 64 bit (AMD64)]

**Additional context**
[Issue is caused by different treatment of `NaN`.](../blob/v0.5.2/pvlib/irradiance.py#L624-L629) specifically if the GHI and DHI are both zero, then if the quotient is undefined or `NaN` - these lines try to use `pd.DataFrame.fillna(0)` which fails for NumPy, and the exception sets the `F` term to zero, instead of just the `NaN` values.

the fix is perhaps:
```python
   F = 1 - ((dhi / ghi) ** 2)
    try:
        # fails with single point input
        F.fillna(0, inplace=True)
    except AttributeError:
        F = np.where(np.isnan(F), 0, F)
```

issue_title: Threading issue with new SQL Observer
issue_body:
Sacred uses threading.Timer objects to execute heartbeat events to update the experiment's database records with the latest buffered IO while the experiment is running.  However, the libraries used by SQL Observer are not thread safe, and this is causing long-running experiments to randomly crash for me.

The exact error differs from run to run.  In some cases, a transaction is created in one thread, and then used on another, which the library will not allow.  In other cases, it tries to use a transaction that has already been rolled back.  In still other cases, experiments run without issue, but the longer-running experiments (10+ minutes) tend to crash more often.

I've temporarily worked around this issue by disabling heartbeat events altogether (by directly editing the Sacred 0.70 library code on the install of my machine).  Once I did that, the library works as intended.

I'm using the SQL Observer with an SQLite3 database on a Windows 10 machine.  I have not tested this on my Mac or Ubuntu machines, but I will update the ticket if I manage to duplicate the issue on those systems as well.

from sacred import Experiment
from sacred.observers import MongoObserver

class NonSerializable:

ex = Experiment('test')

```

issue_title: fp.min/fp.max does not work correctly for -0/+0
issue_body:
For inputs `+0/-0`, `fp.min` and `fp.max` seem to be doing the "wrong" thing currently.

For the following benchmark:
```
; Automatically generated by SBV. Do not edit.
(set-option :produce-models true)
(set-logic QF_FP)
; --- uninterpreted sorts ---
; --- literal constants ---

; --- skolem constants ---
(declare-fun s0 () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53)) ; tracks user variable "r"

(declare-fun s2 () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53)) ; tracks user variable "b"
; --- constant tables ---
; --- skolemized tables ---

   (let ((s4 (fp.isNegative s1)))
   (let ((s5 (and s3 s4)))
   (let ((s6 (fp.isZero s2)))
   (let ((s7 (fp.isPositive s2)))
   (let ((s8 (and s6 s7)))
   (let ((s9 (fp.min s1 s2)))
   (let ((s10 (= s0 s9)))
   (and s5 s8 s10))))))))))
(check-sat)
(get-value (s0))
(get-value (s1))
(get-value (s2))
```

Z3 responds:

```
sat
((s0 (_ +zero 11 53)))
((s1 (_ -zero 11 53)))
((s2 (_ +zero 11 53)))
```

Which suggests `+0, -0, +0` are solutions; but the result should actually be `-0, -0, +0`

A parallel benchmark for fp.max exhibits the same problem:

   (let ((s8 (and s6 s7)))

   (let ((s10 (= s0 s9)))

(check-sat)

Z3 responds:

Which suggests the solution is `-0, +0, -0`; which should actually be `+0, +0, -0`.

issue_title: Term div0 appearing in interpolant leading to segmentation fault in Z3_get_decl_kind
issue_body:
I have an interpolation query over UFs plus integers that I give to Z3 via the Java API, and in the resulting interpolant the term `(div0 0)` appears. I then iterate recursively through the AST using the API, and when I reach this term I get a segmentation fault. `getAstKind` returns `Z3_APP_AST` for this tem, but `getDeclKind(ctx, getAppDecl(ctx, term))` segfaults in `getDeclKind`.

I have two questions here:
First: What is this div0 term and is it expected to appear in interpolants? I only found references to something like this for the theory of bitvectors, but I have only integers. When I try to parse the interpolant that Z3 itself produced with Z3, it tells me that `div0` is an unknown function.

Second: How can I avoid the segmentation fault during formula traversal? What else can I check before calling `getDeclKind` to ensure that the call is legal, if `getAstKind` has told me it is a `Z3_APP_AST`? I believe it crashes because it hits the UNREACHABLE assertion in line 992 of `api_ast.cpp` for this term. Is a case for `div0` missing here?

Interestingly, when dumping the interpolation query to SMTLIB2 format and running the command-line version, I get a different interpolant without this term. (This dump is here: [z3-interpolation-query-div0-smt2.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/346194/z3-interpolation-query-div0-smt2.txt).)

issue_title: Unsat to Sat with Theory of FP Without Variables
issue_body:
On the `master` branch at commit cb6d008da8606e6d7fcbe54c3e7eb10b3f24f01f, consider the following input:

```
(set-logic QF_FP)
(assert (fp.eq (fp.sqrt RTN (fp.max (fp.abs (fp #b1 #b00 #b00)) (fp.neg (fp.neg (fp.max (fp.sqrt RTZ (fp #b0 #b00 #b00)) (fp.sqrt RTP (fp.min (fp.max (fp #b1 #b00 #b01) (fp #b0 #b10 #b01)) (fp #b1 #b00 #b00)))))))) (fp #b0 #b00 #b11)))
(check-sat)
(assert true)
(check-sat)
```

Placing this into a file test.smtlib2 and running with command-line `z3 -smt2 test.smtlib2` yields the following output:

```
unsat
sat
```

Replacing the large FP expression with a simple `false` makes this correctly return `unsat` twice.

It's likely possible to further minimize the expression; I can take another attempt at it myself if desired.

issue_title: Crash on 64-bit build run on Windows 8.1 when linked with /DYNAMICBASE and /HIGHENTROPYVA
issue_body:
In a 64-bit Windows build of z3 with the link options /DYNAMICBASE and /HIGHENTROPYVA, z3 crashes on the following SMT2 input when run on a Windows 8.1 machine:

(set-info :status unknown)
(declare-fun out@0 () (_ BitVec 3))
(declare-fun reset_N@0 () Bool)
(declare-fun reset_N@1 () Bool)
(assert
(let ((?x16 (ite reset_N@0 (ite (bvule (_ bv4 3) out@0) (_ bv1 3) (bvadd out@0 (_ bv1 3))) (_ bv0 3))))
(let (($x21 (bvule (ite reset_N@1 (ite (bvule (_ bv4 3) ?x16) (_ bv1 3) (bvadd ?x16 (_ bv1 3))) (_ bv0 3)) (_ bv4 3))))
(not $x21))))
(check-sat)

The crash does not occur when the link option /HIGHENTROPYVA:NO is used instead, nor does it occur when the same executable is run on Windows 7.  As far as we can tell, the most likely cause of this is a 64 bit pointer->32 bit->64 bit conversion somewhere in the z3 code that is stripping the high-order bits of the pointer, combined with Windows 8 putting more data than Windows 7 does above the 32-bit barrier when /HIGHENTROPYVA is on.  We are concerned about this for our customers who require a 64-bit Windows version of our program to hold all their data - they may run into random crashes if z3 allocates data above the 32-bit barrier, regardless of the link settings used, unless this issue is addressed.  (In other words, our major concern isn't how to run this exact test case without crashing, but making sure that z3 is truly 64-bit clean.)

We are compiling with Visual C++ 2013.

issue_title: Apparently unrelated file changes cause Z3 to spin indefinitely
issue_body:
Apologies in advance for the size of the file at issue (1.6KLOC) ; this problem has proven extremely resistant to minimization.  The file producing this problem is reduced somewhat from an automatically-generated problem related to typechecking in Cryptol.  The file may be found at:
https://gist.github.com/robdockins/b28a08cf6a95d6d5878cbd6ddf321a1f

The linked file causes Z3 to take an extremely long time on the final `check-sat`; I've let it run for more than 30 minutes, but have never seen it finish.

However, if the problem is run in isolation, it is solved almost instantly.  In fact, almost any change in the parts of the file previous to the final `push` frame cause the problem to be solved quickly (e.g., comment out the trivial `check-sat` in line 3, or rename the variable `kn196` into `var1`, or almost anything else).  Moreover, `cvc4` (1.4) and `yices-smt2` (2.5.0) both solve the given file (with no changes) nearly instantly.

This behavior has been observed using Z3 release versions 4.3.2 and 4.4.1 for OSX.

This behavior is extremely perplexing to me.  I have a hunch that it is somehow related to hash-table collision (given the sensitivity to variable names), and/or to some strange interaction with the context stack, but that is pure speculation on my part.

issue_title: Future-proofing goto and takeoff
issue_body:
Last chance for future-proofing DKPY2!

We probably want the namespace `vehicle.commands.goto` and `.takeoff` in the future for waypoints. We may also want `vehicle.takeoff` and `vehicle.goto` as immediate commands with more complex behavior than what we support now.

I propose we change the current functions to `vehicle.simple_goto` and `vehicle.simple_takeoff` so that we

1. Preserve all examples that work now
2. Free up the namespace in the future to have more properly and thoughtfully implemented commands.

This is obviously weird to use the "simple" prefix but it would prevent us from doing a major version bump if we ever wanted to reclaim these function names. We’ll just deprecate (but not remove) the `simple_*` variants when we have suitable replacements.

issue_title: Resampling to EPSG:4326 produces unexpected results
issue_body:
#### Code Sample, a minimal, complete, and verifiable piece of code

Area definition
```ini
REGION: EPSG4326 {
   NAME:    Global equal latitude/longitude grid for global sphere
   PCS_ID:  EPSG:4326
   PCS_DEF: init=EPSG:4326
   XSIZE: 8192
   YSIZE: 4096
   AREA_EXTENT:  (-180.0, -90.0, 180.0, 90.0)
};
```

```python
import glob
import sys
from satpy import Scene

def test_msg(composite):
    fnames = glob.glob("/home/lahtinep/data/satellite/geo/H*MSG3*201801110900*")
    glbl = Scene(reader='hrit_msg', filenames=fnames)
    glbl.load([composite])
    lcl = glbl.resample('EPSG4326')
    lcl.save_dataset(composite, '/tmp/%s_msg.tif' % composite)

def main():
    composite = sys.argv[1]
    test_msg(composite)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```
#### Problem description

Resampling SatPy scenes to global lat/lon grid (EPSG:4326) produces a row of tiny images along the equator.

Single image located at the correct place.
